1â†’# Create Story Workflow
     2â†’
     3â†’**Goal:** Create a comprehensive story file that gives the dev agent everything needed for flawless implementation.
     4â†’
     5â†’**Your Role:** Story context engine that prevents LLM developer mistakes, omissions, or disasters.
     6â†’- Communicate all responses in {communication_language} and generate all documents in {document_output_language}
     7â†’- Your purpose is NOT to copy from epics - it's to create a comprehensive, optimized story file that gives the DEV agent EVERYTHING needed for flawless implementation
     8â†’- COMMON LLM MISTAKES TO PREVENT: reinventing wheels, wrong libraries, wrong file locations, breaking regressions, ignoring UX, vague implementations, lying about completion, not learning from past work
     9â†’- EXHAUSTIVE ANALYSIS REQUIRED: You must thoroughly analyze ALL artifacts to extract critical context - do NOT be lazy or skim! This is the most important function in the entire development process!
    10â†’- UTILIZE SUBPROCESSES AND SUBAGENTS: Use research subagents, subprocesses or parallel processing if available to thoroughly analyze different artifacts simultaneously and thoroughly
    11â†’- SAVE QUESTIONS: If you think of questions or clarifications during analysis, save them for the end after the complete story is written
    12â†’- ZERO USER INTERVENTION: Process should be fully automated except for initial epic/story selection or missing documents
    13â†’
    14â†’---
    15â†’
    16â†’## INITIALIZATION
    17â†’
    18â†’### Configuration Loading
    19â†’
    20â†’Load config from `{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml` and resolve:
    21â†’
    22â†’- `project_name`, `user_name`
    23â†’- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`
    24â†’- `user_skill_level`
    25â†’- `planning_artifacts`, `implementation_artifacts`
    26â†’- `date` as system-generated current datetime
    27â†’
    28â†’### Paths
    29â†’
    30â†’- `sprint_status` = `{implementation_artifacts}/sprint-status.yaml`
    31â†’- `epics_file` = `{planning_artifacts}/epics.md`
    32â†’- `prd_file` = `{planning_artifacts}/prd.md`
    33â†’- `architecture_file` = `{planning_artifacts}/architecture.md`
    34â†’- `ux_file` = `{planning_artifacts}/*ux*.md`
    35â†’- `story_title` = "" (will be elicited if not derivable)
    36â†’- `project_context` = `**/project-context.md` (load if exists)
    37â†’- `default_output_file` = `{implementation_artifacts}/{{story_key}}.md`
    38â†’
    39â†’### Input Files
    40â†’
    41â†’| Input | Description | Path Pattern(s) | Load Strategy |
    42â†’|-------|-------------|------------------|---------------|
    43â†’| prd | PRD (fallback - epics file should have most content) | whole: `{planning_artifacts}/*prd*.md`, sharded: `{planning_artifacts}/*prd*/*.md` | SELECTIVE_LOAD |
    44â†’| architecture | Architecture (fallback - epics file should have relevant sections) | whole: `{planning_artifacts}/*architecture*.md`, sharded: `{planning_artifacts}/*architecture*/*.md` | SELECTIVE_LOAD |
    45â†’| ux | UX design (fallback - epics file should have relevant sections) | whole: `{planning_artifacts}/*ux*.md`, sharded: `{planning_artifacts}/*ux*/*.md` | SELECTIVE_LOAD |
    46â†’| epics | Enhanced epics+stories file with BDD and source hints | whole: `{planning_artifacts}/*epic*.md`, sharded: `{planning_artifacts}/*epic*/*.md` | SELECTIVE_LOAD |
    47â†’
    48â†’---
    49â†’
    50â†’## EXECUTION
    51â†’
    52â†’<workflow>
    53â†’
    54â†’<step n="1" goal="Determine target story">
    55â†’  <check if="{{story_path}} is provided by user or user provided the epic and story number such as 2-4 or 1.6 or epic 1 story 5">
    56â†’    <action>Parse user-provided story path: extract epic_num, story_num, story_title from format like "1-2-user-auth"</action>
    57â†’    <action>Set {{epic_num}}, {{story_num}}, {{story_key}} from user input</action>
    58â†’    <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
    59â†’  </check>
    60â†’
    61â†’  <action>Check if {{sprint_status}} file exists for auto discover</action>
    62â†’  <check if="sprint status file does NOT exist">
    63â†’    <output>ًںڑ« No sprint status file found and no story specified</output>
    64â†’    <output>
    65â†’      **Required Options:**
    66â†’      1. Run `sprint-planning` to initialize sprint tracking (recommended)
    67â†’      2. Provide specific epic-story number to create (e.g., "1-2-user-auth")
    68â†’      3. Provide path to story documents if sprint status doesn't exist yet
    69â†’    </output>
    70â†’    <ask>Choose option [1], provide epic-story number, path to story docs, or [q] to quit:</ask>
    71â†’
    72â†’    <check if="user chooses 'q'">
    73â†’      <action>HALT - No work needed</action>
    74â†’    </check>
    75â†’
    76â†’    <check if="user chooses '1'">
    77â†’      <output>Run sprint-planning workflow first to create sprint-status.yaml</output>
    78â†’      <action>HALT - User needs to run sprint-planning</action>
    79â†’    </check>
    80â†’
    81â†’    <check if="user provides epic-story number">
    82â†’      <action>Parse user input: extract epic_num, story_num, story_title</action>
    83â†’      <action>Set {{epic_num}}, {{story_num}}, {{story_key}} from user input</action>
    84â†’      <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
    85â†’    </check>
    86â†’
    87â†’    <check if="user provides story docs path">
    88â†’      <action>Use user-provided path for story documents</action>
    89â†’      <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
    90â†’    </check>
    91â†’  </check>
    92â†’
    93â†’  <!-- Auto-discover from sprint status only if no user input -->
    94â†’  <check if="no user input provided">
    95â†’    <critical>MUST read COMPLETE {sprint_status} file from start to end to preserve order</critical>
    96â†’    <action>Load the FULL file: {{sprint_status}}</action>
    97â†’    <action>Read ALL lines from beginning to end - do not skip any content</action>
    98â†’    <action>Parse the development_status section completely</action>
    99â†’
   100â†’    <action>Find the FIRST story (by reading in order from top to bottom) where:
   101â†’      - Key matches pattern: number-number-name (e.g., "1-2-user-auth")
   102â†’      - NOT an epic key (epic-X) or retrospective (epic-X-retrospective)
   103â†’      - Status value equals "backlog"
   104â†’    </action>
   105â†’
   106â†’    <check if="no backlog story found">
   107â†’      <output>ًں“‹ No backlog stories found in sprint-status.yaml
   108â†’
   109â†’        All stories are either already created, in progress, or done.
   110â†’
   111â†’        **Options:**
   112â†’        1. Run sprint-planning to refresh story tracking
   113â†’        2. Load PM agent and run correct-course to add more stories
   114â†’        3. Check if current sprint is complete and run retrospective
   115â†’      </output>
   116â†’      <action>HALT</action>
   117â†’    </check>
   118â†’
   119â†’    <action>Extract from found story key (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication"):
   120â†’      - epic_num: first number before dash (e.g., "1")
   121â†’      - story_num: second number after first dash (e.g., "2")
   122â†’      - story_title: remainder after second dash (e.g., "user-authentication")
   123â†’    </action>
   124â†’    <action>Set {{story_id}} = "{{epic_num}}.{{story_num}}"</action>
   125â†’    <action>Store story_key for later use (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication")</action>
   126â†’
   127â†’    <!-- Mark epic as in-progress if this is first story -->
   128â†’    <action>Check if this is the first story in epic {{epic_num}} by looking for {{epic_num}}-1-* pattern</action>
   129â†’    <check if="this is first story in epic {{epic_num}}">
   130â†’      <action>Load {{sprint_status}} and check epic-{{epic_num}} status</action>
   131â†’      <action>If epic status is "backlog" â†’ update to "in-progress"</action>
   132â†’      <action>If epic status is "contexted" (legacy status) â†’ update to "in-progress" (backward compatibility)</action>
   133â†’      <action>If epic status is "in-progress" â†’ no change needed</action>
   134â†’      <check if="epic status is 'done'">
   135â†’        <output>ًںڑ« ERROR: Cannot create story in completed epic</output>

   139â†’        <output>2. Create a new epic for additional work</output>
   140â†’        <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
   141â†’      </check>
   142â†’      <check if="epic status is not one of: backlog, contexted, in-progress, done">
   143â†’        <output>ًںڑ« ERROR: Invalid epic status '{{epic_status}}'</output>
   144â†’        <output>Epic {{epic_num}} has invalid status. Expected: backlog, in-progress, or done</output>
   145â†’        <output>Please fix sprint-status.yaml manually or run sprint-planning to regenerate</output>
   146â†’        <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
   147â†’      </check>

   149â†’    </check>
   150â†’

file.content:
# Create Story Workflow

**Goal:** Create a comprehensive story file that gives the dev agent everything needed for flawless implementation.

**Your Role:** Story context engine that prevents LLM developer mistakes, omissions, or disasters.
- Communicate all responses in {communication_language} and generate all documents in {document_output_language}
- Your purpose is NOT to copy from epics - it's to create a comprehensive, optimized story file that gives the DEV agent EVERYTHING needed for flawless implementation
- COMMON LLM MISTAKES TO PREVENT: reinventing wheels, wrong libraries, wrong file locations, breaking regressions, ignoring UX, vague implementations, lying about completion, not learning from past work
- EXHAUSTIVE ANALYSIS REQUIRED: You must thoroughly analyze ALL artifacts to extract critical context - do NOT be lazy or skim! This is the most important function in the entire development process!
- UTILIZE SUBPROCESSES AND SUBAGENTS: Use research subagents, subprocesses or parallel processing if available to thoroughly analyze different artifacts simultaneously and thoroughly
- SAVE QUESTIONS: If you think of questions or clarifications during analysis, save them for the end after the complete story is written
- ZERO USER INTERVENTION: Process should be fully automated except for initial epic/story selection or missing documents

---

## INITIALIZATION

### Configuration Loading

Load config from `{project-root}/_bmad/bmm/config.yaml` and resolve:

- `project_name`, `user_name`
- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`
- `user_skill_level`
- `planning_artifacts`, `implementation_artifacts`
- `date` as system-generated current datetime

### Paths

- `sprint_status` = `{implementation_artifacts}/sprint-status.yaml`
- `epics_file` = `{planning_artifacts}/epics.md`
- `prd_file` = `{planning_artifacts}/prd.md`
- `architecture_file` = `{planning_artifacts}/architecture.md`
- `ux_file` = `{planning_artifacts}/*ux*.md`
- `story_title` = "" (will be elicited if not derivable)
- `project_context` = `**/project-context.md` (load if exists)
- `default_output_file` = `{implementation_artifacts}/{{story_key}}.md`

### Input Files

| Input | Description | Path Pattern(s) | Load Strategy |
|-------|-------------|------------------|---------------|
| prd | PRD (fallback - epics file should have most content) | whole: `{planning_artifacts}/*prd*.md`, sharded: `{planning_artifacts}/*prd*/*.md` | SELECTIVE_LOAD |
| architecture | Architecture (fallback - epics file should have relevant sections) | whole: `{planning_artifacts}/*architecture*.md`, sharded: `{planning_artifacts}/*architecture*/*.md` | SELECTIVE_LOAD |
| ux | UX design (fallback - epics file should have relevant sections) | whole: `{planning_artifacts}/*ux*.md`, sharded: `{planning_artifacts}/*ux*/*.md` | SELECTIVE_LOAD |
| epics | Enhanced epics+stories file with BDD and source hints | whole: `{planning_artifacts}/*epic*.md`, sharded: `{planning_artifacts}/*epic*/*.md` | SELECTIVE_LOAD |

---

## EXECUTION

<workflow>

<step n="1" goal="Determine target story">
  <check if="{{story_path}} is provided by user or user provided the epic and story number such as 2-4 or 1.6 or epic 1 story 5">
    <action>Parse user-provided story path: extract epic_num, story_num, story_title from format like "1-2-user-auth"</action>
    <action>Set {{epic_num}}, {{story_num}}, {{story_key}} from user input</action>
    <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
  </check>

  <action>Check if {{sprint_status}} file exists for auto discover</action>
  <check if="sprint status file does NOT exist">
    <output>ًںڑ« No sprint status file found and no story specified</output>
    <output>
      **Required Options:**
      1. Run `sprint-planning` to initialize sprint tracking (recommended)
      2. Provide specific epic-story number to create (e.g., "1-2-user-auth")
      3. Provide path to story documents if sprint status doesn't exist yet
    </output>
    <ask>Choose option [1], provide epic-story number, path to story docs, or [q] to quit:</ask>

    <check if="user chooses 'q'">
      <action>HALT - No work needed</action>
    </check>

    <check if="user chooses '1'">
      <output>Run sprint-planning workflow first to create sprint-status.yaml</output>
      <action>HALT - User needs to run sprint-planning</action>
    </check>

    <check if="user provides epic-story number">
      <action>Parse user input: extract epic_num, story_num, story_title</action>
      <action>Set {{epic_num}}, {{story_num}}, {{story_key}} from user input</action>
      <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
    </check>

    <check if="user provides story docs path">
      <action>Use user-provided path for story documents</action>
      <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
    </check>
  </check>

  <!-- Auto-discover from sprint status only if no user input -->
  <check if="no user input provided">
    <critical>MUST read COMPLETE {sprint_status} file from start to end to preserve order</critical>
    <action>Load the FULL file: {{sprint_status}}</action>
    <action>Read ALL lines from beginning to end - do not skip any content</action>
    <action>Parse the development_status section completely</action>

    <action>Find the FIRST story (by reading in order from top to bottom) where:
      - Key matches pattern: number-number-name (e.g., "1-2-user-auth")
      - NOT an epic key (epic-X) or retrospective (epic-X-retrospective)
      - Status value equals "backlog"
    </action>

    <check if="no backlog story found">
      <output>ًں“‹ No backlog stories found in sprint-status.yaml

        All stories are either already created, in progress, or done.

        **Options:**
        1. Run sprint-planning to refresh story tracking
        2. Load PM agent and run correct-course to add more stories
        3. Check if current sprint is complete and run retrospective
      </output>
      <action>HALT</action>
    </check>

    <action>Extract from found story key (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication"):
      - epic_num: first number before dash (e.g., "1")
      - story_num: second number after first dash (e.g., "2")
      - story_title: remainder after second dash (e.g., "user-authentication")
    </action>
    <action>Set {{story_id}} = "{{epic_num}}.{{story_num}}"</action>
    <action>Store story_key for later use (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication")</action>

    <!-- Mark epic as in-progress if this is first story -->
    <action>Check if this is the first story in epic {{epic_num}} by looking for {{epic_num}}-1-* pattern</action>
    <check if="this is first story in epic {{epic_num}}">
      <action>Load {{sprint_status}} and check epic-{{epic_num}} status</action>
      <action>If epic status is "backlog" â†’ update to "in-progress"</action>
      <action>If epic status is "contexted" (legacy status) â†’ update to "in-progress" (backward compatibility)</action>
      <action>If epic status is "in-progress" â†’ no change needed</action>
      <check if="epic status is 'done'">
        <output>ًںڑ« ERROR: Cannot create story in completed epic</output>
        <output>Epic {{epic_num}} is marked as 'done'. All stories are complete.</output>
        <output>If you need to add more work, either:</output>
        <output>1. Manually change epic status back to 'in-progress' in sprint-status.yaml</output>
        <output>2. Create a new epic for additional work</output>
        <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
      </check>
      <check if="epic status is not one of: backlog, contexted, in-progress, done">
        <output>ًںڑ« ERROR: Invalid epic status '{{epic_status}}'</output>
        <output>Epic {{epic_num}} has invalid status. Expected: backlog, in-progress, or done</output>
        <output>Please fix sprint-status.yaml manually or run sprint-planning to regenerate</output>
        <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
      </check>
      <output>ًں“ٹ Epic {{epic_num}} status updated to in-progress</output>
    </check>

    <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
  </check>
  <action>Load the FULL file: {{sprint_status}}</action>
  <action>Read ALL lines from beginning to end - do not skip any content</action>
  <action>Parse the development_status section completely</action>

  <action>Find the FIRST story (by reading in order from top to bottom) where:
    - Key matches pattern: number-number-name (e.g., "1-2-user-auth")
    - NOT an epic key (epic-X) or retrospective (epic-X-retrospective)
    - Status value equals "backlog"
  </action>

  <check if="no backlog story found">
    <output>No backlog stories found in sprint-status.yaml

      All stories are either already created, in progress, or done.

      **Options:**
      1. Run sprint-planning to refresh story tracking
      2. Load PM agent and run correct-course to add more stories
      3. Check if current sprint is complete and run retrospective
    </output>
    <action>HALT</action>
  </check>

  <action>Extract from found story key (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication"):
    - epic_num: first number before dash (e.g., "1")
    - story_num: second number after first dash (e.g., "2")
    - story_title: remainder after second dash (e.g., "user-authentication")
  </action>
  <action>Set {{story_id}} = "{{epic_num}}.{{story_num}}"</action>
  <action>Store story_key for later use (e.g., "1-2-user-authentication")</action>

  <!-- Mark epic as in-progress if this is first story -->
  <action>Check if this is the first story in epic {{epic_num}} by looking for {{epic_num}}-1-* pattern</action>
  <check if="this is first story in epic {{epic_num}}">
    <action>Load {{sprint_status}} and check epic-{{epic_num}} status</action>
    <action>If epic status is "backlog" â†’ update to "in-progress"</action>
    <action>If epic status is "contexted" (legacy status) â†’ update to "in-progress" (backward compatibility)</action>
    <action>If epic status is "in-progress" â†’ no change needed</action>
    <check if="epic status is 'done'">
      <output>ERROR: Cannot create story in completed epic</output>
      <output>Epic {{epic_num}} is marked as 'done'. All stories are complete.</output>
      <output>If you need to add more work, either:</output>
      <output>1. Manually change epic status back to 'in-progress' in sprint-status.yaml</output>
      <output>2. Create a new epic for additional work</output>
      <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
    </check>
    <check if="epic status is not one of: backlog, contexted, in-progress, done">
      <output>ERROR: Invalid epic status '{{epic_status}}'</output>
      <output>Epic {{epic_num}} has invalid status. Expected: backlog, in-progress, or done</output>
      <output>Please fix sprint-status.yaml manually or run sprint-planning to regenerate</output>
      <action>HALT - Cannot proceed</action>
    </check>
    <output>Epic {{epic_num}} status updated to in-progress</output>
  </check>

  <action>GOTO step 2a</action>
</step>

<step n="2" goal="Load and analyze core artifacts">
  <critical>ًں”¬ EXHAUSTIVE ARTIFACT ANALYSIS - This is where you prevent future developer mistakes!</critical>

  <!-- Load all available content through discovery protocol -->
  <action>Read fully and follow `./discover-inputs.md` to load all input files</action>
  <note>Available content: {epics_content}, {prd_content}, {architecture_content}, {ux_content},
  {project_context}</note>

  <!-- Analyze epics file for story foundation -->
  <action>From {epics_content}, extract Epic {{epic_num}} complete context:</action> **EPIC ANALYSIS:** - Epic
  objectives and business value - ALL stories in this epic for cross-story context - Our specific story's requirements, user story
  statement, acceptance criteria - Technical requirements and constraints - Dependencies on other stories/epics - Source hints pointing to
  original documents <!-- Extract specific story requirements -->
  <action>Extract our story ({{epic_num}}-{{story_num}}) details:</action> **STORY FOUNDATION:** - User story statement
  (As a, I want, so that) - Detailed acceptance criteria (already BDD formatted) - Technical requirements specific to this story -
  Business context and value - Success criteria <!-- Previous story analysis for context continuity -->
  <check if="story_num > 1">
    <action>Find {{previous_story_num}}: scan {implementation_artifacts} for the story file in epic {{epic_num}} with the highest story number less than {{story_num}}</action>
    <action>Load previous story file: {implementation_artifacts}/{{epic_num}}-{{previous_story_num}}-*.md</action> **PREVIOUS STORY INTELLIGENCE:** -
  Dev notes and learnings from previous story - Review feedback and corrections needed - Files that were created/modified and their
  patterns - Testing approaches that worked/didn't work - Problems encountered and solutions found - Code patterns established <action>Extract
  all learnings that could impact current story implementation</action>
  </check>

  <!-- Git intelligence for previous work patterns -->
  <check
    if="previous story exists AND git repository detected">
    <action>Get last 5 commit titles to understand recent work patterns</action>
    <action>Analyze 1-5 most recent commits for relevance to current story:
      - Files created/modified
      - Code patterns and conventions used
      - Library dependencies added/changed
      - Architecture decisions implemented
      - Testing approaches used
    </action>
    <action>Extract actionable insights for current story implementation</action>
  </check>
</step>

<step n="3" goal="Architecture analysis for developer guardrails">
  <critical>ًںڈ—ï¸ڈ ARCHITECTURE INTELLIGENCE - Extract everything the developer MUST follow!</critical> **ARCHITECTURE DOCUMENT ANALYSIS:** <action>Systematically
  analyze architecture content for story-relevant requirements:</action>

  <!-- Load architecture - single file or sharded -->
  <check if="architecture file is single file">
    <action>Load complete {architecture_content}</action>
  </check>
  <check if="architecture is sharded to folder">
    <action>Load architecture index and scan all architecture files</action>
  </check> **CRITICAL ARCHITECTURE EXTRACTION:** <action>For
  each architecture section, determine if relevant to this story:</action> - **Technical Stack:** Languages, frameworks, libraries with
  versions - **Code Structure:** Folder organization, naming conventions, file patterns - **API Patterns:** Service structure, endpoint
  patterns, data contracts - **Database Schemas:** Tables, relationships, constraints relevant to story - **Security Requirements:**
  Authentication patterns, authorization rules - **Performance Requirements:** Caching strategies, optimization patterns - **Testing
  Standards:** Testing frameworks, coverage expectations, test patterns - **Deployment Patterns:** Environment configurations, build
  processes - **Integration Patterns:** External service integrations, data flows <action>Extract any story-specific requirements that the
  developer MUST follow</action>
  <action>Identify any architectural decisions that override previous patterns</action>
</step>

<step n="4" goal="Web research for latest technical specifics">
  <critical>ًںŒگ ENSURE LATEST TECH KNOWLEDGE - Prevent outdated implementations!</critical> **WEB INTELLIGENCE:** <action>Identify specific
  technical areas that require latest version knowledge:</action>

  <!-- Check for libraries/frameworks mentioned in architecture -->
  <action>From architecture analysis, identify specific libraries, APIs, or
  frameworks</action>
  <action>For each critical technology, research latest stable version and key changes:
    - Latest API documentation and breaking changes
    - Security vulnerabilities or updates
    - Performance improvements or deprecations
    - Best practices for current version
  </action>
  **EXTERNAL CONTEXT INCLUSION:** <action>Include in story any critical latest information the developer needs:
    - Specific library versions and why chosen
    - API endpoints with parameters and authentication
    - Recent security patches or considerations
    - Performance optimization techniques
    - Migration considerations if upgrading
  </action>
</step>

<step n="5" goal="Create comprehensive story file">
  <critical>ًں“‌ CREATE ULTIMATE STORY FILE - The developer's master implementation guide!</critical>

  <action>Initialize from template.md:
  {default_output_file}</action>
  <template-output file="{default_output_file}">story_header</template-output>

  <!-- Story foundation from epics analysis -->
  <template-output
    file="{default_output_file}">story_requirements</template-output>

  <!-- Developer context section - MOST IMPORTANT PART -->
  <template-output file="{default_output_file}">
  developer_context_section</template-output> **DEV AGENT GUARDRAILS:** <template-output file="{default_output_file}">
  technical_requirements</template-output>
  <template-output file="{default_output_file}">architecture_compliance</template-output>
  <template-output
    file="{default_output_file}">library_framework_requirements</template-output>
  <template-output file="{default_output_file}">
  file_structure_requirements</template-output>
  <template-output file="{default_output_file}">testing_requirements</template-output>

  <!-- Previous story intelligence -->
  <check
    if="previous story learnings available">
    <template-output file="{default_output_file}">previous_story_intelligence</template-output>
  </check>

  <!-- Git intelligence -->
  <check
    if="git analysis completed">
    <template-output file="{default_output_file}">git_intelligence_summary</template-output>
  </check>

  <!-- Latest technical specifics -->
  <check if="web research completed">
    <template-output file="{default_output_file}">latest_tech_information</template-output>
  </check>

  <!-- Project context reference -->
  <template-output
    file="{default_output_file}">project_context_reference</template-output>

  <!-- Final status update -->
  <template-output file="{default_output_file}">
  story_completion_status</template-output>

  <!-- CRITICAL: Set status to ready-for-dev -->
  <action>Set story Status to: "ready-for-dev"</action>
  <action>Add completion note: "Ultimate
  context engine analysis completed - comprehensive developer guide created"</action>
</step>

<step n="6" goal="Update sprint status and finalize">
  <action>Validate the newly created story file {default_output_file} against `./checklist.md` and apply any required fixes before finalizing</action>
  <action>Save story document unconditionally</action>

  <!-- Update sprint status -->
  <check if="sprint status file exists">
    <action>Update {{sprint_status}}</action>
    <action>Load the FULL file and read all development_status entries</action>
    <action>Find development_status key matching {{story_key}}</action>
    <action>Verify current status is "backlog" (expected previous state)</action>
    <action>Update development_status[{{story_key}}] = "ready-for-dev"</action>
    <action>Update last_updated field to current date</action>
    <action>Save file, preserving ALL comments and structure including STATUS DEFINITIONS</action>
  </check>

  <action>Report completion</action>
  <output>**ًںژ¯ ULTIMATE BMad Method STORY CONTEXT CREATED, {user_name}!**

    **Story Details:**
    - Story ID: {{story_id}}
    - Story Key: {{story_key}}
    - File: {{story_file}}
    - Status: ready-for-dev

    **Next Steps:**
    1. Review the comprehensive story in {{story_file}}
    2. Run dev agents `dev-story` for optimized implementation
    3. Run `code-review` when complete (auto-marks done)
    4. Optional: If Test Architect module installed, run `/bmad:tea:automate` after `dev-story` to generate guardrail tests

    **The developer now has everything needed for flawless implementation!**
  </output>
</step>

</workflow>

file.content:
# Discover Inputs Protocol

**Objective:** Intelligently load project files (whole or sharded) based on the workflow's Input Files configuration.

**Prerequisite:** Only execute this protocol if the workflow defines an Input Files section. If no input file patterns are configured, skip this entirely.

---

## Step 1: Parse Input File Patterns

- Read the Input Files table from the workflow configuration.
- For each input group (prd, architecture, epics, ux, etc.), note the **load strategy** if specified.

## Step 2: Load Files Using Smart Strategies

For each pattern in the Input Files table, work through the following substeps in order:

### 2a: Try Sharded Documents First

If a sharded pattern exists for this input, determine the load strategy (defaults to **FULL_LOAD** if not specified), then apply the matching strategy:

#### FULL_LOAD Strategy

Load ALL files in the sharded directory. Use this for PRD, Architecture, UX, brownfield docs, or whenever the full picture is needed.

1. Use the glob pattern to find ALL `.md` files (e.g., `{planning_artifacts}/*architecture*/*.md`).
2. Load EVERY matching file completely.
3. Concatenate content in logical order: `index.md` first if it exists, then alphabetical.
4. Store the combined result in a variable named `{pattern_name_content}` (e.g., `{architecture_content}`).

#### SELECTIVE_LOAD Strategy

Load a specific shard using a template variable. Example: used for epics with `{{epic_num}}`.

1. Check for template variables in the sharded pattern (e.g., `{{epic_num}}`).
2. If the variable is undefined, ask the user for the value OR infer it from context.
3. Resolve the template to a specific file path.
4. Load that specific file.
5. Store in variable: `{pattern_name_content}`.

#### INDEX_GUIDED Strategy

Load index.md, analyze the structure and description of each doc in the index, then intelligently load relevant docs.

**DO NOT BE LAZY** -- use best judgment to load documents that might have relevant information, even if there is only a 5% chance of relevance.

1. Load `index.md` from the sharded directory.
2. Parse the table of contents, links, and section headers.
3. Analyze the workflow's purpose and objective.
4. Identify which linked/referenced documents are likely relevant.
   - *Example:* If the workflow is about authentication and the index shows "Auth Overview", "Payment Setup", "Deployment" -- load the auth docs, consider deployment docs, skip payment.
5. Load all identified relevant documents.
6. Store combined content in variable: `{pattern_name_content}`.

**When in doubt, LOAD IT** -- context is valuable, and being thorough is better than missing critical info.

---

After applying the matching strategy, mark the pattern as **RESOLVED** and move to the next pattern.

### 2b: Try Whole Document if No Sharded Found

If no sharded matches were found OR no sharded pattern exists for this input:

1. Attempt a glob match on the "whole" pattern (e.g., `{planning_artifacts}/*prd*.md`).
2. If matches are found, load ALL matching files completely (no offset/limit).
3. Store content in variable: `{pattern_name_content}` (e.g., `{prd_content}`).
4. Mark pattern as **RESOLVED** and move to the next pattern.

### 2c: Handle Not Found

If no matches were found for either sharded or whole patterns:

1. Set `{pattern_name_content}` to empty string.
2. Note in session: "No {pattern_name} files found" -- this is not an error, just unavailable. Offer the user a chance to provide the file.

## Step 3: Report Discovery Results

List all loaded content variables with file counts. Example:

```
OK Loaded {prd_content} from 5 sharded files: prd/index.md, prd/requirements.md, ...
OK Loaded {architecture_content} from 1 file: Architecture.md

```

This gives the workflow transparency into what context is available.

1â†’---
     2â†’stepsCompleted: [step-01-validate-prerequisites, step-02-design-epics, step-03-create-stories, step-04-final-validation]
     3â†’inputDocuments:
     4â†’  - prd.md
     5â†’  - architecture.md
     6â†’  - ux-design-specification.md
     7â†’---
     8â†’
     9â†’# Metrics - Epic Breakdown
    10â†’
    11â†’## Overview
    12â†’
    13â†’This document provides the complete epic and story breakdown for Metrics, decomposing the requirements from the PRD, UX Design if it exists, and Architecture requirements into implementable stories.
    14â†’
    15â†’## Requirements Inventory
    16â†’
    17â†’### Functional Requirements
    18â†’
    19â†’- FR1: User can view the current BTC/USD price, updated in real-time
    20â†’- FR2: User can see price updates reflected in the browser tab title
    21â†’- FR3: User can see a visual indication of price direction (up/down) on each update
    22â†’- FR4: User can view the 24-hour price change as both a percentage and absolute value
    23â†’- FR5: User can distinguish positive and negative price changes via color coding
    24â†’- FR6: User can view the current BTC market capitalization
    25â†’- FR7: System refreshes supplementary market data (24h change, market cap) periodically without user action
    26â†’- FR8: User can view a sparkline chart showing BTC price over the last 24 hours
    27â†’- FR9: User can hover over the chart to see the price and time at a specific data point
    28â†’- FR10: Chart displays hourly data points for the 24-hour period
    29â†’- FR11: System connects to the primary WebSocket data source (Binance) on page load
    30â†’- FR12: System falls back to a secondary WebSocket source (CoinCap) if the primary fails
    31â†’- FR13: System automatically reconnects when a WebSocket connection drops
    32â†’- FR14: User can see a visual indicator when the data connection is lost or reconnecting
    33â†’- FR15: User can see a stale data indicator when displayed price may be outdated
    34â†’- FR16: System falls back to REST API polling when WebSocket is unavailable
    35â†’- FR17: User sees a dark-themed interface with crypto-aesthetic styling on page load
    36â†’- FR18: User experiences no flash of unstyled or light-themed content on initial load
    37â†’- FR19: User sees smooth entry animations when data first loads
    38â†’- FR20: User sees no layout shift as dynamic content populates
    39â†’- FR21: User can view all dashboard data on desktop screens with a horizontal layout
    40â†’- FR22: User can view all dashboard data on mobile screens with a vertically stacked layout
    41â†’- FR23: Dashboard is usable and readable across all supported modern browsers
    42â†’
    43â†’### NonFunctional Requirements
    44â†’
    45â†’- NFR1: Page load (DOMContentLoaded) completes in < 2 seconds on a 4G connection
    46â†’- NFR2: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) occurs in < 1.5 seconds
    47â†’- NFR3: Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) = 0 â€” no visible layout shift during data hydration
    48â†’- NFR4: Price updates render to DOM within 1 second of WebSocket message receipt
    49â†’- NFR5: All animations run at 60 FPS with no dropped frames
    50â†’- NFR6: Lighthouse performance score â‰¥ 90
    51â†’- NFR7: Total hydrated JavaScript bundle < 100KB gzipped
    52â†’- NFR8: Chart library (lightweight-charts) < 50KB gzipped
    53â†’- NFR9: Binance WebSocket connection establishes within 500ms of island hydration under normal network conditions
    54â†’- NFR10: WebSocket reconnection uses exponential backoff starting at 1 second, capping at 30 seconds
    55â†’- NFR11: CoinGecko REST API polling interval â‰¥ 60 seconds to stay within free tier rate limits (30 req/min)
    56â†’- NFR12: System tolerates CoinGecko API unavailability by displaying last known data with a timestamp
    57â†’- NFR13: Binance 24-hour connection expiry handled transparently as a standard reconnect cycle
    58â†’- NFR14: Fallback from Binance to CoinCap completes without user intervention
    59â†’- NFR15: Dashboard remains functional (displays last known data) when all external APIs are unreachable
    60â†’- NFR16: No JavaScript errors or white screens on WebSocket disconnection
    61â†’- NFR17: Browser tab remains stable during extended sessions (no memory leaks over 24+ hours)
    62â†’
    63â†’### Additional Requirements
    64â†’
    65â†’- Starter template: `npm create astro@latest -- --template minimal --typescript strict` â€” specified in Architecture as Epic 1 Story 1
    66â†’- Tailwind CSS v4 via `@tailwindcss/vite` plugin with CSS-first `@theme` configuration (no `tailwind.config.js`)
    67â†’- Nano Stores (~1KB) as the reactive state layer shared across Astro islands (Astro's officially recommended approach)
    68â†’- Vanilla TypeScript islands â€” no UI framework; islands use Astro `<script>` tags with `client:load` for direct DOM updates
    69â†’- WebSocket ConnectionManager as a singleton managing the entire WebSocket lifecycle with fallback chain
    70â†’- `theme-constants.ts` for lightweight-charts JS API color sync with Tailwind `@theme` tokens (duplicated values, acceptable for v1)
    71â†’- Shared TypeScript interfaces in `src/lib/types.ts`: `PriceTick`, `MarketData`, `ChartPoint`, `ConnectionStatus`
    72â†’- Unidirectional data flow: External API â†’ Service â†’ Store â†’ Component â†’ DOM
    73â†’- Memory leak prevention: listener cleanup on reconnect, chart data capped at 24 entries, timer reference management with `AbortController`
    74â†’- ESLint (`eslint-plugin-astro`) + Prettier (`prettier-plugin-astro`) + Vitest from first commit
    75â†’- Playwright for e2e smoke test ("page loads, price appears")
    76â†’- Package manager: npm (explicit choice)
    77â†’- Node 22+ required (Astro 6.x)
    78â†’- Co-located test files with `.test.ts` suffix
    79â†’- No barrel files (`utils/index.ts`) â€” import directly from specific modules
    80â†’- Recursive `setTimeout` for REST polling (not `setInterval`) to prevent drift on slow responses
    81â†’
    82â†’### UX Design Requirements
    83â†’
    84â†’- UX-DR1: Implement design token system â€” semantic color tokens (`bg-base` #0a0a0f, `bg-card` #12121a, `bg-card-hover` #1a1a2e, `accent-primary` #00f5ff cyan, `accent-secondary` #8b5cf6 purple, `accent-glow` rgba(0,245,255,0.15), `price-up` #22c55e, `price-down` #ef4444, `price-flash-up` #00f5ff, `price-flash-down` #ef4444, `text-primary` #f0f0f0, `text-secondary` #a0a0b0, `text-muted` #606070, `status-live` #22c55e, `status-reconnecting` #f59e0b, `status-stale` #ef4444) via Tailwind `@theme` directives in `src/styles/global.css`
    85â†’- UX-DR2: Implement typography system â€” JetBrains Mono (`font-price`) for all numerical data, Inter (`font-label`) for labels/status text. Type scale: price 48px/36px weight 600, values 20-24px weight 500, card labels 12px Inter weight 400 `text-secondary`, status 11px Inter weight 400 `text-muted`, chart tooltip 13px JetBrains Mono. Line heights: 1.1 prices, 1.4 labels, 1.3 status
    86â†’- UX-DR3: Implement glassmorphism card pattern (`.card-glass`) â€” `bg-card` background + `backdrop-blur(12px)` + 1px border `rgba(255,255,255,0.08)` + 16px border-radius + optional `accent-secondary` glow (`box-shadow: 0 0 20px rgba(139,92,246,0.1)`)
    87â†’- UX-DR4: Implement Price Card component â€” label "Bitcoin BTC/USD", live price at 48px desktop/36px mobile, change row with arrow symbol (â†‘/â†“) + percentage (+2.3%) + absolute (+$1,534.22) in `price-up`/`price-down` colors. States: Live (color flash 400ms), Stale (badge), Loading (label visible, data fades in 300ms)
    88â†’- UX-DR5: Implement Market Cap Card component â€” label "Market Cap", value abbreviated ($1.34T) at 24px weight 500, secondary "24h Range" label with range value at 14px. Silent 60s refresh, no flash animation
    89â†’- UX-DR6: Implement Chart Card component â€” lightweight-charts with line color `accent-primary` (#00f5ff), area gradient (rgba(0,245,255,0.1) top, transparent bottom), grid lines rgba(255,255,255,0.03), crosshair rgba(255,255,255,0.3), transparent background. Hover crosshair with price+time tooltip (13px JetBrains Mono). Draw animation 500ms. Full width spanning both grid columns on desktop
    90â†’- UX-DR7: Implement Status Indicator component â€” 6px colored dot + state text at 11px `text-muted`, centered bottom of viewport. States: Live (green dot + "Live"), Reconnecting (amber pulsing dot 1.5s cycle + "Reconnecting..."), Stale (red static dot + "Connection lost"), Fallback (green dot + "Live â€” CoinCap"). State transitions fade 300ms
    91â†’- UX-DR8: Implement Page Shell â€” full-viewport `bg-base` (#0a0a0f) background, CSS Grid `1.5fr 1fr` desktop / single column mobile, 640px breakpoint, max-width 720px container, vertical centering on desktop, top-aligned on mobile. Spacing: 32px page padding desktop, 16px mobile, 24px card gaps
    92â†’- UX-DR9: Implement animation system â€” data fade-in 300ms ease-out, chart draw 500ms ease-out, price tick flash 400ms ease-out, state transitions 300ms ease-in-out, reconnecting pulse 1.5s ease-in-out, card hover glow 300ms ease. No animation exceeds 500ms. No bounce/spring/overshoot. All respect `prefers-reduced-motion` (0ms durations). Price flash throttled to max 1 per 200ms
    93â†’- UX-DR10: Implement FOUC prevention â€” set `bg-base` (#0a0a0f) on `<html>` element directly, dark theme as first paint. Empty glassmorphism cards with labels as the loading state (no spinners, no skeletons). Reserved space for all dynamic content (CLS = 0)
    94â†’- UX-DR11: Implement responsive design â€” desktop-first with single 640px breakpoint (`sm:` Tailwind prefix). Price 48pxâ†’36px, grid collapses to single column. No `vh` units on mobile (use `dvh` or `min-h-screen`). Chart auto-resizes via `ResizeObserver`. No features hidden on mobile. CSS-only responsive logic
    95â†’- UX-DR12: Implement accessibility â€” `<section aria-label="Bitcoin price">` with `<output aria-live="polite">` for price, `<section aria-label="Market data">` for market cap, `<section aria-label="24 hour price chart">` with `aria-hidden="true"` on chart canvas, `<div role="status" aria-live="polite">` for Status Indicator, `<html lang="en">`. Direction arrows (â†‘/â†“) alongside color coding. All text meets AA contrast minimum
    96â†’
    97â†’### FR Coverage Map
    98â†’
    99â†’- FR1: Epic 2 â€” Live BTC/USD price display
   100â†’- FR2: Epic 2 â€” Price in browser tab title
   101â†’- FR3: Epic 2 â€” Price direction indicator (up/down)
   102â†’- FR4: Epic 2 â€” 24h change (percentage + absolute)
   103â†’- FR5: Epic 2 â€” Color-coded positive/negative changes
   104â†’- FR6: Epic 3 â€” Market cap display
   105â†’- FR7: Epic 3 â€” Periodic market data refresh
   106â†’- FR8: Epic 3 â€” 24h sparkline chart
   107â†’- FR9: Epic 3 â€” Chart hover tooltip (price + time)
   108â†’- FR10: Epic 3 â€” Hourly chart data points
   109â†’- FR11: Epic 2 â€” Binance WebSocket connection on load
   110â†’- FR12: Epic 4 â€” CoinCap fallback WebSocket
   111â†’- FR13: Epic 4 â€” Auto-reconnection on drop
   112â†’- FR14: Epic 4 â€” Reconnecting visual indicator
   113â†’- FR15: Epic 4 â€” Stale data indicator
   114â†’- FR16: Epic 4 â€” REST polling fallback
   115â†’- FR17: Epic 1 â€” Dark-themed crypto aesthetic
   116â†’- FR18: Epic 1 â€” No FOUC
   117â†’- FR19: Epic 2 â€” Entry animations
   118â†’- FR20: Epic 1 â€” No layout shift
   119â†’- FR21: Epic 1 â€” Desktop horizontal layout
   120â†’- FR22: Epic 1 â€” Mobile stacked layout
   121â†’- FR23: Epic 1 â€” Cross-browser support
   122â†’
   123â†’## Epic List
   124â†’
   125â†’### Epic 1: Project Foundation & Dashboard Shell
   126â†’The user sees a polished, responsive dark crypto dashboard that loads instantly â€” the visual identity and layout are complete, ready for live data.
   127â†’**FRs covered:** FR17, FR18, FR20, FR21, FR22, FR23
   128â†’
   129â†’### Epic 2: Live Bitcoin Price Display
   130â†’The user can glance at the dashboard (or tab title) and instantly know the current BTC price, its direction, and 24h change â€” updated in real-time.
   131â†’**FRs covered:** FR1, FR2, FR3, FR4, FR5, FR11, FR19
   132â†’
   133â†’### Epic 3: Market Context & Price Chart
   134â†’The user gets the full market picture â€” market cap, 24h range, and a 24h sparkline chart with hover tooltips â€” completing the dashboard's information offering.
   135â†’**FRs covered:** FR6, FR7, FR8, FR9, FR10
   136â†’
   137â†’### Epic 4: Connection Resilience & Reliability
   138â†’The dashboard never breaks â€” WebSocket failures are handled silently with fallback sources, automatic reconnection, and clear stale data indication. The user can trust the data is always there.
   139â†’**FRs covered:** FR12, FR13, FR14, FR15, FR16
   140â†’
   141â†’## Epic 1: Project Foundation & Dashboard Shell
   142â†’
   143â†’The user sees a polished, responsive dark crypto dashboard that loads instantly â€” the visual identity and layout are complete, ready for live data.
   144â†’
   145â†’### Story 1.1: Project Initialization & Tooling Setup
   146â†’
   147â†’As a developer,
   148â†’I want a fully scaffolded Astro project with TypeScript, Tailwind, and code quality tooling,
   149â†’So that I can start building components with a consistent, well-configured dev environment from the first commit.
   150â†’
   151â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   152â†’
   153â†’**Given** a clean project directory
   154â†’**When** the initialization commands are run
   155â†’**Then** Astro 6.x is installed with the minimal template and TypeScript strict mode
   156â†’**And** Tailwind CSS v4 is configured via `@tailwindcss/vite` plugin in `astro.config.mjs`
   157â†’**And** `lightweight-charts`, `@fontsource/jetbrains-mono`, `@fontsource/inter`, and `nanostores` are installed
   158â†’**And** ESLint with `eslint-plugin-astro`, Prettier with `prettier-plugin-astro`, and Vitest are configured
   159â†’**And** the project structure follows Architecture spec: `src/pages/`, `src/layouts/`, `src/components/`, `src/lib/stores/`, `src/lib/services/`, `src/lib/utils/`, `src/styles/`
   160â†’**And** `src/lib/types.ts` defines `PriceTick`, `MarketData`, `ChartPoint`, and `ConnectionStatus` interfaces
   161â†’**And** `npm run dev` starts the Astro dev server successfully
   162â†’**And** `npm run build` produces a static output in `dist/`
   163â†’
   164â†’### Story 1.2: Design Token System & Typography
   165â†’
   166â†’As a developer,
   167â†’I want a complete design token system with semantic colors, typography, and shared theme constants,
   168â†’So that all components use a consistent dark crypto visual identity and the chart library stays in sync.
   169â†’
   170â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   171â†’
   172â†’**Given** the Astro project from Story 1.1
   173â†’**When** `src/styles/global.css` is created with Tailwind `@theme` directives
   174â†’**Then** all semantic color tokens are defined: `bg-base` (#0a0a0f), `bg-card` (#12121a), `bg-card-hover` (#1a1a2e), `accent-primary` (#00f5ff), `accent-secondary` (#8b5cf6), `accent-glow` (rgba(0,245,255,0.15)), `price-up` (#22c55e), `price-down` (#ef4444), `price-flash-up` (#00f5ff), `price-flash-down` (#ef4444), `text-primary` (#f0f0f0), `text-secondary` (#a0a0b0), `text-muted` (#606070), `status-live` (#22c55e), `status-reconnecting` (#f59e0b), `status-stale` (#ef4444)
   175â†’**And** JetBrains Mono is configured as `font-price` and Inter as `font-label` via `@fontsource` imports
   176â†’**And** the type scale is defined: price 48px weight 600, values 20-24px weight 500, labels 12px weight 400, status 11px weight 400
   177â†’**And** `src/lib/theme-constants.ts` exports duplicated color values for lightweight-charts JS API configuration
   178â†’**And** `.card-glass`, `.price-text`, and `.status-badge` `@apply` compositions are defined (max 3)
   179â†’**And** CSS custom properties for runtime animation values (`--price-flash-up`, `--price-flash-down`) are defined in `:root`
   180â†’**And** tokens are usable via Tailwind utility classes in any component
   181â†’
   182â†’### Story 1.3: Page Shell with Responsive Layout
   183â†’
   184â†’As a user,
   185â†’I want the dashboard to load instantly with a dark background and a responsive card grid layout,
   186â†’So that I never see a flash of white and the page looks polished on both desktop and mobile.
   187â†’
   188â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   189â†’
   190â†’**Given** a user navigates to the dashboard URL
   191â†’**When** the page loads
   192â†’**Then** `<html>` has `lang="en"` and `bg-base` (#0a0a0f) set directly to prevent FOUC (FR18)
   193â†’**And** `Layout.astro` renders a full-viewport dark background with a centered content container (max-width 720px)
   194â†’**And** the layout uses CSS Grid with `grid-template-columns: 1.5fr 1fr` and 24px gaps on desktop (â‰¥640px) (FR21)
   195â†’**And** the layout collapses to a single-column stack with 16px horizontal padding on mobile (<640px) (FR22)
   196â†’**And** the container is vertically centered on desktop and top-aligned on mobile
   197â†’**And** no `vh` units are used on mobile â€” `dvh` or `min-h-screen` is used instead
   198â†’**And** responsive behavior is CSS-only with no JavaScript (Tailwind `sm:` breakpoint)
   199â†’**And** the page renders correctly on Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge latest 2 versions (FR23)
   200â†’**And** `index.astro` imports `Layout.astro` and serves as the single page entry
   201â†’
   202â†’### Story 1.4: Empty Dashboard Cards
   203â†’
   204â†’As a user,
   205â†’I want to see the dashboard layout with labeled card placeholders immediately on page load,
   206â†’So that the page feels complete and no layout shift occurs when data arrives later.
   207â†’
   208â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   209â†’
   210â†’**Given** a user loads the dashboard
   211â†’**When** the static shell renders (before any data)
   212â†’**Then** four glassmorphism cards are visible: Price Card, Market Cap Card, Chart Card, Status Indicator area
   213â†’**And** each card uses the `.card-glass` pattern: `bg-card` + `backdrop-blur(12px)` + 1px border rgba(255,255,255,0.08) + 16px border-radius
   214â†’**And** Price Card displays label "Bitcoin BTC/USD" in `font-label`, `text-secondary`, 12px
   215â†’**And** Market Cap Card displays label "Market Cap" in `font-label`, `text-secondary`, 12px
   216â†’**And** Chart Card displays label "24h Chart" in `font-label`, `text-secondary`, 12px and spans full grid width on desktop
   217â†’**And** each card has reserved space (fixed height) for its data values so CLS = 0 when data populates (FR20)
   218â†’**And** Price Card has `<section aria-label="Bitcoin price">` with `<output aria-live="polite">` placeholder
   219â†’**And** Market Cap Card has `<section aria-label="Market data">`
   220â†’**And** Chart Card has `<section aria-label="24 hour price chart">`
   221â†’**And** Status Indicator area has `<div role="status" aria-live="polite">` at the bottom of the viewport
   222â†’**And** the dark-themed crypto aesthetic is visually complete â€” near-black background, glassmorphism cards, neon accents visible (FR17)
   223â†’
   224â†’## Epic 2: Live Bitcoin Price Display
   225â†’
   226â†’The user can glance at the dashboard (or tab title) and instantly know the current BTC price, its direction, and 24h change â€” updated in real-time.
   227â†’
   228â†’### Story 2.1: Nano Stores & Number Formatting Utilities
   229â†’
   230â†’As a developer,
   231â†’I want reactive stores and consistent number formatting helpers in place,
   232â†’So that all components share a single source of truth for data and display values identically.
   233â†’
   234â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   235â†’
   236â†’**Given** the project structure from Epic 1
   237â†’**When** the store and utility files are created
   238â†’**Then** `src/lib/stores/price-store.ts` exports `priceStore` as `atom<PriceTick | null>(null)`
   239â†’**And** `src/lib/stores/status-store.ts` exports `statusStore` as `atom<ConnectionStatus>('connecting')`
   240â†’**And** `src/lib/stores/market-store.ts` exports `marketStore` as `atom<MarketData | null>(null)`
   241â†’**And** `src/lib/stores/chart-store.ts` exports `chartStore` as `atom<ChartPoint[]>([])`
   242â†’**And** every store is typed with explicit generics and initializes to `null` or empty (never undefined)
   243â†’**And** `src/lib/utils/format-utils.ts` exports: `formatPrice()` â†’ `$68,432.17`, `formatPercent()` â†’ `+2.3%` or `-1.8%`, `formatAbsoluteChange()` â†’ `+$1,534.22`, `formatMarketCap()` â†’ `$1.34T`, `formatRange()` â†’ `$66.9k â€” $68.9k`, `formatChartTooltip()` â†’ `$68,432.17 at 14:30`
   244â†’**And** all formatters use `Intl.NumberFormat` as the base â€” no manual string building
   245â†’**And** `format-utils.test.ts` covers all formatters with positive, negative, zero, and edge cases
   246â†’**And** tests pass via `npm run test`
   247â†’
   248â†’### Story 2.2: WebSocket Connection Manager (Binance)
   249â†’
   250â†’As a user,
   251â†’I want the dashboard to connect to a live Bitcoin data source automatically on page load,
   252â†’So that I see real-time price updates without any action.
   253â†’
   254â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   255â†’
   256â†’**Given** the dashboard page loads and islands hydrate
   257â†’**When** the ConnectionManager initializes
   258â†’**Then** it connects to Binance WebSocket at `wss://stream.binance.com:9443/ws/btcusdt@trade` (FR11)
   259â†’**And** raw Binance messages (`{ p: "68432.17", ... }`) are normalized to `PriceTick` format: `{ price: number, timestamp: number, direction: 'up' | 'down' | 'neutral' }`
   260â†’**And** direction is computed by comparing to the previous price
   261â†’**And** normalized `PriceTick` is written to `priceStore`
   262â†’**And** `statusStore` is updated to `'live'` on successful connection
   263â†’**And** `statusStore` is set to `'connecting'` during initial connection attempt
   264â†’**And** the ConnectionManager is a singleton â€” only one WebSocket connection exists at any time
   265â†’**And** `connection-manager.ts` never exposes raw WebSocket data to consumers
   266â†’**And** old WebSocket listeners are cleaned up before creating new connections (memory leak prevention)
   267â†’**And** services initialize via a `<script>` block in `Layout.astro`
   268â†’**And** `connection-manager.test.ts` covers message normalization and direction computation
   269â†’
   270â†’### Story 2.3: Live Price Card with Direction Indicator
   271â†’
   272â†’As a user,
   273â†’I want to see the current BTC price updating live with visual direction feedback,
   274â†’So that I can glance at the dashboard and instantly know the price and whether it's moving up or down.
   275â†’
   276â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   277â†’
   278â†’**Given** the Price Card island is hydrated and priceStore has data
   279â†’**When** a new PriceTick arrives in priceStore
   280â†’**Then** the price displays in `font-price` at 48px desktop / 36px mobile, weight 600, `text-primary` color (FR1)
   281â†’**And** a direction arrow is shown: `â†‘` in `price-up` green for up, `â†“` in `price-down` red for down (FR3, FR5)
   282â†’**And** the price text flashes cyan (`price-flash-up`) on price increase or red (`price-flash-down`) on decrease, fading to white over 400ms ease-out (UX-DR9)
   283â†’**And** flash frequency is throttled to max 1 per 200ms to prevent rapid flashing
   284â†’**And** 24h change displays as percentage (`+2.3%`) and absolute value (`+$1,534.22`) using format-utils (FR4)
   285â†’**And** 24h change values use `price-up` green for positive and `price-down` red for negative (FR5)
   286â†’**And** all number formatting goes through `format-utils.ts` â€” no inline formatting
   287â†’**And** on initial data load, the price fades in with a 300ms ease-out animation (FR19)
   288â†’**And** all animations respect `prefers-reduced-motion` (instant transitions when enabled)
   289â†’**And** the `<output aria-live="polite">` element updates so screen readers announce price changes
   290â†’
   291â†’### Story 2.4: Tab Title Price Updates
   292â†’
   293â†’As a user,
   294â†’I want the browser tab title to show the current BTC price,
   295â†’So that I can see the price without switching to the Metrics tab.
   296â†’
   297â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   298â†’
   299â†’**Given** the dashboard is live and priceStore is receiving updates
   300â†’**When** a new PriceTick arrives
   301â†’**Then** the browser tab title updates to show the formatted price, e.g. `$68,432.17 â€” Metrics` (FR2)
   302â†’**And** the tab title uses the same `formatPrice()` function from format-utils for consistency
   303â†’**And** the tab title updates on every price tick (not throttled separately from the display)
   304â†’**And** if priceStore is `null` (no data yet), the tab title shows `Metrics` without a price
   305â†’
   306â†’## Epic 3: Market Context & Price Chart
   307â†’
   308â†’The user gets the full market picture â€” market cap, 24h range, and a 24h sparkline chart with hover tooltips â€” completing the dashboard's information offering.
   309â†’
   310â†’### Story 3.1: Market Data Service (CoinGecko REST Polling)
   311â†’
   312â†’As a user,
   313â†’I want the dashboard to fetch market data automatically in the background,
   314â†’So that I see up-to-date market cap, 24h change, and chart data without any action.
   315â†’
   316â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   317â†’
   318â†’**Given** the dashboard page loads and services initialize
   319â†’**When** the MarketDataService starts
   320â†’**Then** it fetches market cap, 24h change (percentage + absolute), 24h high/low, and hourly historical price data from CoinGecko REST API
   321â†’**And** `marketStore` is populated with a `MarketData` object: `{ marketCap, change24h, changePercent24h, high24h, low24h }`
   322â†’**And** `chartStore` is populated with an array of `ChartPoint` objects: `{ time: number, value: number }` for hourly data
   323â†’**And** `chartStore` is capped at 24 entries maximum â€” oldest point shifted when appending new data
   324â†’**And** polling uses recursive `setTimeout` (not `setInterval`) with a 60-second interval (NFR11)
   325â†’**And** on CoinGecko API failure, last known store values are retained â€” stores are never cleared (NFR12)
   326â†’**And** API errors are logged via `console.warn()` with endpoint and status code â€” no user-facing error UI
   327â†’**And** timer references are stored and cleared before scheduling new timers (memory leak prevention)
   328â†’**And** `market-data-service.test.ts` covers data mapping, store updates, and error retention behavior
   329â†’
   330â†’### Story 3.2: Market Cap Card
   331â†’
   332â†’As a user,
   333â†’I want to see the current Bitcoin market cap and 24h price range at a glance,
   334â†’So that I have broader market context alongside the live price.
   335â†’
   336â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   337â†’
   338â†’**Given** the Market Cap Card island is hydrated and marketStore has data
   339â†’**When** marketStore updates with new MarketData
   340â†’**Then** market cap displays as an abbreviated value (e.g. `$1.34T`) in `font-price`, `text-primary`, 24px weight 500 (FR6)
   341â†’**And** "Market Cap" label is shown in `font-label`, `text-secondary`, 12px
   342â†’**And** 24h range displays as `$66.9k â€” $68.9k` in `font-price`, `text-primary`, 14px
   343â†’**And** "24h Range" secondary label is shown in `font-label`, `text-secondary`, 12px
   344â†’**And** all values use `formatMarketCap()` and `formatRange()` from format-utils â€” no inline formatting
   345â†’**And** values update silently every 60s with no flash animation (FR7)
   346â†’**And** on initial data load, values fade in with 300ms ease-out animation
   347â†’**And** when marketStore is `null` (no data yet), labels are visible but value areas are empty (reserved space)
   348â†’
   349â†’### Story 3.3: 24h Sparkline Chart with Hover Tooltip
   350â†’
   351â†’As a user,
   352â†’I want to see a 24-hour price trend chart that I can hover for details,
   353â†’So that I can quickly read the trend shape and check specific price points.
   354â†’
   355â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   356â†’
   357â†’**Given** the Chart Card island is hydrated and chartStore has hourly data
   358â†’**When** chartStore populates with ChartPoint array
   359â†’**Then** a lightweight-charts area series renders with line color `accent-primary` (#00f5ff) (FR8)
   360â†’**And** area fill uses gradient: rgba(0,245,255,0.1) top to transparent bottom
   361â†’**And** chart grid lines are rgba(255,255,255,0.03), crosshair rgba(255,255,255,0.3)
   362â†’**And** chart background is transparent (card provides the background)
   363â†’**And** all chart colors are sourced from `theme-constants.ts` â€” no hardcoded values in component
   364â†’**And** chart displays hourly data points for the 24-hour period (FR10)
   365â†’**And** on hover, a crosshair appears with a tooltip showing price and time formatted as `$68,432.17 at 14:30` using `formatChartTooltip()` (FR9)
   366â†’**And** on initial data load, the chart draws left-to-right with a 500ms ease-out animation (UX-DR6)
   367â†’**And** the chart auto-resizes via lightweight-charts built-in resize handling on viewport change
   368â†’**And** the chart card spans full grid width (`grid-column: 1 / -1`) on desktop
   369â†’**And** chart canvas has `aria-hidden="true"` â€” price data is accessible via Price Card
   370â†’**And** animation respects `prefers-reduced-motion` (instant draw when enabled)
   371â†’**And** when chartStore updates with new data, the chart appends points without full redraw or flicker
   372â†’
   373â†’## Epic 4: Connection Resilience & Reliability
   374â†’
   375â†’The dashboard never breaks â€” WebSocket failures are handled silently with fallback sources, automatic reconnection, and clear stale data indication. The user can trust the data is always there.
   376â†’
   377â†’### Story 4.1: WebSocket Reconnection with Exponential Backoff
   378â†’
   379â†’As a user,
   380â†’I want the dashboard to automatically recover from connection drops without my intervention,
   381â†’So that the live price feed resumes seamlessly after network interruptions.
   382â†’
   383â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   384â†’
   385â†’**Given** the ConnectionManager has an active Binance WebSocket connection
   386â†’**When** the WebSocket connection drops (error, close, or network failure)
   387â†’**Then** `statusStore` transitions to `'reconnecting'` (FR14)
   388â†’**And** reconnection attempts begin with exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, 30s cap (NFR10)
   389â†’**And** on successful reconnection, `statusStore` transitions back to `'live'`
   390â†’**And** the previous WebSocket instance is fully closed (`ws.close()`) and its reference nulled before creating a new connection (memory leak prevention, NFR17)
   391â†’**And** no JavaScript errors or white screens occur during disconnection or reconnection (NFR16)
   392â†’**And** Binance 24-hour connection expiry is handled as a standard disconnect/reconnect cycle â€” no special logic (NFR13)
   393â†’**And** the last known price remains displayed in the Price Card during reconnection (NFR15)
   394â†’**And** `connection-manager.test.ts` covers backoff timing, listener cleanup, and reconnection state transitions
   395â†’
   396â†’### Story 4.2: CoinCap Fallback & REST Polling Fallback
   397â†’
   398â†’As a user,
   399â†’I want the dashboard to try alternative data sources if the primary one fails,
   400â†’So that I always see price data regardless of which service is available.
   401â†’
   402â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   403â†’
   404â†’**Given** the ConnectionManager fails to connect or reconnect to Binance
   405â†’**When** Binance WebSocket is unavailable after reconnection attempts
   406â†’**Then** the ConnectionManager attempts to connect to CoinCap WebSocket at `wss://ws.coincap.io/prices?assets=bitcoin` (FR12)
   407â†’**And** CoinCap messages (`{ bitcoin: "68432.17" }`) are normalized to the same `PriceTick` format
   408â†’**And** on successful CoinCap connection, `statusStore` transitions to `'fallback'`
   409â†’**And** fallback from Binance to CoinCap completes without user intervention (NFR14)
   410â†’**And** if both Binance and CoinCap WebSockets fail, the system falls back to REST polling via the MarketDataService for price data (FR16)
   411â†’**And** during REST-only mode, `statusStore` reflects `'stale'` if no update received within 30 seconds
   412â†’**And** the dashboard remains functional displaying last known data when all external APIs are unreachable (NFR15)
   413â†’**And** when a higher-priority source becomes available again, the ConnectionManager reconnects to it
   414â†’**And** `connection-manager.test.ts` covers the full fallback chain: Binance â†’ CoinCap â†’ REST
   415â†’
   416â†’### Story 4.3: Status Indicator & Stale Data Badge
   417â†’
   418â†’As a user,
   419â†’I want to see a subtle connection status indicator and know when displayed data may be outdated,
   420â†’So that I can trust the data when it's live and be aware when it's stale.
   421â†’
   422â†’**Acceptance Criteria:**
   423â†’
   424â†’**Given** the Status Indicator island is hydrated and subscribes to statusStore
   425â†’**When** statusStore changes value
   426â†’**Then** the indicator displays at the bottom center of the viewport as a 6px dot + text at 11px `text-muted` (UX-DR7)
   427â†’**And** Live state shows: green dot (`status-live`) + "Live" text â€” barely visible, quiet good state
   428â†’**And** Reconnecting state shows: amber dot (`status-reconnecting`) + "Reconnecting..." text with dot pulsing animation (1.5s ease-in-out cycle, opacity 0.4â€“1.0) (FR14)
   429â†’**And** Stale state shows: red dot (`status-stale`) + "Connection lost" text, static (no animation)
   430â†’**And** Fallback state shows: green dot (`status-live`) + "Live â€” CoinCap" text
   431â†’**And** state transitions fade over 300ms ease-in-out â€” no abrupt switches
   432â†’**And** when statusStore is `'stale'` (no price update for >30 seconds), a "Stale" badge appears on the Price Card in `status-stale` color (FR15)
   433â†’**And** the stale badge does not hide or replace the price value â€” it's an addition
   434â†’**And** when connection restores and a new price tick arrives, the stale badge fades out over 300ms
   435â†’**And** all animations respect `prefers-reduced-motion` (instant transitions, no pulsing)
   436â†’**And** the Status Indicator uses `<div role="status" aria-live="polite">` so screen readers announce state changes
   437â†’
