    19â†’# Product Requirements Document - Metrics
    20â†’
    21â†’**Author:** Dmytro
    22â†’**Date:** 2026-03-25
    23â†’
    24â†’## Executive Summary
    25â†’
    26â†’Metrics is a single-page Bitcoin dashboard built on Astro JS that delivers real-time BTC price data through a polished, dark crypto interface. It solves a specific problem: checking the Bitcoin price today means navigating bloated exchange platforms or news aggregators packed with irrelevant coins, ads, and trading interfaces. Metrics provides the answer in one glance â€” live price via WebSocket, 24-hour change, market cap, and a sparkline chart â€” with zero accounts, zero configuration, and zero noise.
    27â†’
    28â†’The target user is a developer (initially Dmytro) who wants a fast, beautiful, always-open BTC tab. The product is designed around three pillars: **speed** (sub-2s page load, sub-1s price updates), **aesthetics** (dark crypto visual identity with glassmorphism, neon accents, and monospaced typography), and **focus** (one asset, one page, one purpose).
    29â†’
    30â†’Deliberate constraint is the feature. Where CoinGecko, CoinMarketCap, and exchange UIs compete on breadth, Metrics competes on depth of simplicity. The core insight: the best dashboard is the one you don't have to think about. It's built for someone who wants to *know* the price, not *trade* it. Astro's Islands Architecture hydrates only the interactive components (price ticker, chart) while the shell stays static HTML â€” a page that loads like a static site but updates like a live app.
    31â†’
    32â†’## Project Classification
    33â†’
    34â†’- **Project Type:** Web application (SPA-like with Astro Islands Architecture)
    35â†’- **Domain:** Fintech (cryptocurrency data visualization)
    36â†’- **Complexity:** Low â€” read-only public API consumption, no user data, no transactions, no regulatory requirements
    37â†’- **Project Context:** Greenfield â€” new product, no existing codebase
    38â†’
    39â†’## Success Criteria
    40â†’
    41â†’### User Success
    42â†’
    43â†’- **Instant awareness:** User opens a tab and knows the current BTC price within 1 second â€” no scanning, no clicking, no waiting
    44â†’- **Pinned tab worthy:** The dashboard is reliable and beautiful enough to earn a permanent spot in the browser tab bar
    45â†’- **Zero learning curve:** No onboarding, no configuration, no accounts â€” it just works on first visit
    46â†’
    47â†’### Business Success
    48â†’
    49â†’- **Personal utility:** Dmytro uses Metrics daily as his primary BTC price check, replacing CoinGecko/Google
    50â†’- **Craft quality:** The project demonstrates polished frontend engineering â€” suitable for sharing as open-source or a portfolio piece
    51â†’- **Deployable:** Can be moved from local dev to public hosting (Vercel/Netlify) with minimal effort when ready
    52â†’
    53â†’### Technical Success
    54â†’
    55â†’Specific targets defined in Non-Functional Requirements (NFR1â€“NFR17). Key benchmarks:
    56â†’
    57â†’- Lighthouse performance â‰¥ 90, accessibility â‰¥ 80
    58â†’- WebSocket connection uptime > 99% during active browser sessions
    59â†’- Total JS bundle (hydrated islands only) < 100KB gzipped
    60â†’- Chart library < 50KB gzipped
    61â†’
    62â†’## User Journeys
    63â†’
    64â†’### Journey 1: The Morning Price Check (Happy Path)
    65â†’
    66â†’**Dmytro, developer, morning coffee ritual.**
    67â†’
    68â†’It's 8:15 AM. Dmytro grabs his coffee and opens his laptop. Among his pinned browser tabs, Metrics is already there. He glances at it â€” **$68,432.17**, green arrow, **+2.3% in 24h**. The sparkline shows a gentle upward trend since yesterday afternoon. Market cap sits at $1.34T. He knows everything he needs to know in under 2 seconds. He moves on to his work. No clicks, no scrolling, no cognitive load. The tab title even shows the price, so sometimes he doesn't even need to switch to it.
    69â†’
    70â†’**Capabilities revealed:** Real-time price display, 24h change indicator, sparkline chart, market cap, tab title with price, instant page load from pinned tab state.
    71â†’
    72â†’### Journey 2: The Volatile Day (Edge Case â€” Reconnection & Stale Data)
    73â†’
    74â†’**Dmytro, mid-afternoon, market is moving fast.**
    75â†’
    76â†’Bitcoin just dropped 8% in an hour. Dmytro switches to his Metrics tab to watch it move. The price is ticking down live â€” **$62,100... $61,980... $61,850**. Then his WiFi blips. A subtle indicator appears: **"Reconnecting..."** in muted text. Within seconds, the WebSocket reconnects via exponential backoff and the live feed resumes. He didn't have to refresh. If the connection had stayed down longer, the price would have shown a stale-data badge so he'd know not to trust it, and the app would have fallen back to REST polling for updates every 60 seconds.
    77â†’
    78â†’**Capabilities revealed:** WebSocket reconnection with exponential backoff, stale data visual indicator, REST polling fallback, high-frequency price update rendering without layout shift.
    79â†’
    80â†’### Journey 3: The Fresh Visit (First-Time Load)
    81â†’
    82â†’**Dmytro opens Metrics for the first time on a new machine.**
    83â†’
    84â†’He types the local dev URL into his browser. The page loads in under 1.5 seconds â€” dark background fills the screen first (no flash of white), then the static shell renders immediately: labels, card outlines, layout. Within 500ms, the WebSocket connects and the price appears with a smooth fade-in. The sparkline draws itself with a quick animation. No loading spinners, no skeleton screens â€” just a fast, elegant entrance. He bookmarks it, pins the tab, and it becomes part of his daily setup.
    85â†’
    86â†’**Capabilities revealed:** Fast initial page load (static-first Astro), WebSocket connection initialization, graceful data hydration without layout shift, dark theme renders immediately (no FOUC), smooth entry animations.
    87â†’
    88â†’### Journey 4: The Mobile Glance (Responsive Edge Case)
    89â†’
    90â†’**Dmytro, on his phone, quick check while out.**
    91â†’
    92â†’He opens Metrics on his phone browser. The layout stacks vertically â€” price prominently on top, 24h change below, then the sparkline chart fills the width, market cap at the bottom. Everything is readable, touch-friendly, and the dark theme looks sharp on OLED. The WebSocket connects and the price starts updating live. Same experience, smaller screen.
    93â†’
    94â†’**Capabilities revealed:** Responsive design (desktop-first with mobile stacking), touch-friendly layout, OLED-friendly dark theme, full functionality on mobile browsers.
    95â†’
    96â†’### Journey Requirements Summary
    97â†’
    98â†’| Capability | J1 | J2 | J3 | J4 |
    99â†’|---|---|---|---|---|
   100â†’| Live BTC price via WebSocket | âœ“ | âœ“ | âœ“ | âœ“ |
   101â†’| 24h change (% and absolute) | âœ“ | âœ“ | âœ“ | âœ“ |
   102â†’| Market cap display | âœ“ | âœ“ | âœ“ | âœ“ |
   103â†’| 24h sparkline chart | âœ“ | âœ“ | âœ“ | âœ“ |
   104â†’| Tab title with price | âœ“ | | | |
   105â†’| WebSocket reconnection | | âœ“ | | |
   106â†’| Stale data indicator | | âœ“ | | |
   107â†’| REST polling fallback | | âœ“ | | |
   108â†’| Fast initial load (< 2s) | | | âœ“ | |
   109â†’| Graceful data hydration | | | âœ“ | |
   110â†’| No FOUC (dark theme first) | | | âœ“ | |
   111â†’| Entry animations | | | âœ“ | |
   112â†’| Responsive mobile layout | | | | âœ“ |
   113â†’
   114â†’## Technical Architecture
   115â†’
   116â†’### Astro Islands Architecture
   117â†’
   118â†’Single-page Astro JS application. The page shell is static HTML (MPA pattern) with two hydrated client islands: the WebSocket price ticker and the sparkline chart. This gives the performance benefits of a static site with the interactivity of a live dashboard.
   119â†’
   120â†’- **Rendering model:** Astro static-first with `client:load` directives for interactive islands (price ticker, chart component)
   121â†’- **Real-time data:** WebSocket connections managed within hydrated islands only â€” no SSR or server-side WebSocket handling
   122â†’- **State management:** Minimal â€” component-local state within each island. No global state store needed. Price data flows: WebSocket â†’ island component â†’ DOM
   123â†’- **Asset strategy:** Tailwind CSS compiled at build time; JetBrains Mono and Inter loaded via `@fontsource` packages or self-hosted for performance
   124â†’
   125â†’### Browser Support
   126â†’
   127â†’- Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge (latest 2 versions each)
   128â†’- No IE11 or legacy browser support
   129â†’- `backdrop-filter` (glassmorphism) supported across all targets â€” no fallback needed
   130â†’
   131â†’### Accessibility
   132â†’
   133â†’- Semantic HTML structure (headings, landmarks, ARIA labels where meaningful)
   134â†’- Sufficient color contrast for price text against dark backgrounds
   135â†’- No formal WCAG compliance target â€” best-effort with sensible defaults
   136â†’
   137â†’### Implementation Constraints
   138â†’
   139â†’- **No SEO requirements** â€” personal dashboard, not indexed
   140â†’- **No SSR needed** â€” fully static build with client-side hydration
   141â†’- **No routing** â€” single page, no client-side router
   142â†’- **No backend** â€” all data sourced from public WebSocket and REST APIs client-side
   143â†’- **Dev server:** Astro dev server for local development; production build outputs static files
   144â†’
   145â†’## Project Scoping & Phased Development
   146â†’
   147â†’### MVP Strategy
   148â†’
   149â†’**MVP Approach:** Experience MVP â€” the minimum feature set that delivers the full "open tab, know the price" experience with visual polish. No feature stubs or placeholder UI. Everything shipped in v1 works completely and looks finished.
   150â†’
   151â†’**Resource Requirements:** Solo developer (Dmytro). Astro JS, Tailwind CSS, and TypeScript skills. No backend or DevOps needed for v1.
   152â†’
   153â†’**Core User Journeys Supported:** All four (J1â€“J4) â€” full support.
   154â†’
   155â†’### MVP Feature Set (Phase 1)
   156â†’
   157â†’- Live BTC/USD price via Binance WebSocket with CoinCap fallback
   158â†’- 24h price change (percentage and absolute) via CoinGecko REST
   159â†’- Market cap display via CoinGecko REST (polled every 60s)
   160â†’- 24h sparkline chart using lightweight-charts (TradingView), hourly data, hover tooltips
   161â†’- Dark crypto aesthetic: near-black backgrounds, neon accents, glassmorphism cards, JetBrains Mono / Inter typography
   162â†’- Tab title showing current price
   163â†’- WebSocket reconnection with exponential backoff
   164â†’- Stale data indicator on disconnect
   165â†’- REST polling fallback when WebSocket is unavailable
   166â†’- Responsive layout (desktop-first, mobile stacking)
   167â†’- No FOUC â€” dark theme renders immediately
   168â†’- Smooth entry animations for data hydration
   169â†’- Local development setup with Astro dev server
   170â†’
   171â†’### Key Technical Decisions
   172â†’
   173â†’- **Chart library:** lightweight-charts (TradingView) â€” finance-native, ~40KB gzipped, fits bundle constraint
   174â†’- **WebSocket fallback strategy:** Try Binance first, fall back to CoinCap on failure. No auto-detection or geo-check â€” simple sequential fallback acceptable for v1
   175â†’- **Binance 24h expiry:** Reconnection logic handles this as a normal disconnect/reconnect cycle
   176â†’
   177â†’### Phase 2 (Growth)
   178â†’
   179â†’- Static hosting deployment (Vercel/Netlify) with production build
   180â†’- Extended chart timeframes (7d, 30d)
   181â†’- Additional fiat currency options (EUR, GBP)
   182â†’- PWA support for mobile home screen install
   183â†’
   184â†’### Phase 3 (Expansion)
   185â†’
   186â†’- Multi-asset support (ETH, SOL, etc.)
   187â†’- Personal portfolio tracking
   188â†’- Reusable dark-theme Astro component library
   189â†’- Public open-source release
   190â†’
   191â†’### Risk Mitigation
   192â†’
   193â†’- *Binance WebSocket geo-blocked:* CoinCap fallback provides equivalent functionality. Acceptable latency trade-off for v1.
   194â†’- *CoinGecko rate limiting:* 60s polling interval stays well within 30 req/min free tier. If hit, degrade gracefully by showing last known data with timestamp.
   195â†’- *lightweight-charts Astro compatibility:* Library is framework-agnostic vanilla JS â€” low integration risk with Astro client islands.
   196â†’- *Resource:* Solo developer project. Tight scope minimizes risk. No external dependencies requiring agreements.
   197â†’
   198â†’## Functional Requirements
   199â†’
   200â†’### Real-Time Price Display
   201â†’
   202â†’- FR1: User can view the current BTC/USD price, updated in real-time
   203â†’- FR2: User can see price updates reflected in the browser tab title
   204â†’- FR3: User can see a visual indication of price direction (up/down) on each update
   205â†’
   206â†’### Market Data
   207â†’
   208â†’- FR4: User can view the 24-hour price change as both a percentage and absolute value
   209â†’- FR5: User can distinguish positive and negative price changes via color coding
   210â†’- FR6: User can view the current BTC market capitalization
   211â†’- FR7: System refreshes supplementary market data (24h change, market cap) periodically without user action
   212â†’
   213â†’### Price Chart
   214â†’
   215â†’- FR8: User can view a sparkline chart showing BTC price over the last 24 hours
   216â†’- FR9: User can hover over the chart to see the price and time at a specific data point
   217â†’- FR10: Chart displays hourly data points for the 24-hour period
   218â†’
   219â†’### Connection Resilience
   220â†’
   221â†’- FR11: System connects to the primary WebSocket data source (Binance) on page load
   222â†’- FR12: System falls back to a secondary WebSocket source (CoinCap) if the primary fails
   223â†’- FR13: System automatically reconnects when a WebSocket connection drops
   224â†’- FR14: User can see a visual indicator when the data connection is lost or reconnecting
   225â†’- FR15: User can see a stale data indicator when displayed price may be outdated
   226â†’- FR16: System falls back to REST API polling when WebSocket is unavailable
   227â†’
   228â†’### Visual Presentation
   229â†’
   230â†’- FR17: User sees a dark-themed interface with crypto-aesthetic styling on page load
   231â†’- FR18: User experiences no flash of unstyled or light-themed content on initial load
   232â†’- FR19: User sees smooth entry animations when data first loads
   233â†’- FR20: User sees no layout shift as dynamic content populates
   234â†’
   235â†’### Responsive Experience
   236â†’
   237â†’- FR21: User can view all dashboard data on desktop screens with a horizontal layout
   238â†’- FR22: User can view all dashboard data on mobile screens with a vertically stacked layout
   239â†’- FR23: Dashboard is usable and readable across all supported modern browsers
   240â†’
   241â†’## Non-Functional Requirements
   242â†’
   243â†’### Performance
   244â†’
   245â†’- NFR1: Page load (DOMContentLoaded) completes in < 2 seconds on a 4G connection
   246â†’- NFR2: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) occurs in < 1.5 seconds
   247â†’- NFR3: Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) = 0 â€” no visible layout shift during data hydration
   248â†’- NFR4: Price updates render to DOM within 1 second of WebSocket message receipt
   249â†’- NFR5: All animations run at 60 FPS with no dropped frames
   250â†’- NFR6: Lighthouse performance score â‰¥ 90
   251â†’- NFR7: Total hydrated JavaScript bundle < 100KB gzipped
   252â†’- NFR8: Chart library (lightweight-charts) < 50KB gzipped
   253â†’
   254â†’### Integration
   255â†’
   256â†’- NFR9: Binance WebSocket connection establishes within 500ms of island hydration under normal network conditions
   257â†’- NFR10: WebSocket reconnection uses exponential backoff starting at 1 second, capping at 30 seconds
   258â†’- NFR11: CoinGecko REST API polling interval â‰¥ 60 seconds to stay within free tier rate limits (30 req/min)
   259â†’- NFR12: System tolerates CoinGecko API unavailability by displaying last known data with a timestamp
   260â†’- NFR13: Binance 24-hour connection expiry handled transparently as a standard reconnect cycle
   261â†’- NFR14: Fallback from Binance to CoinCap completes without user intervention
   262â†’
   263â†’### Reliability
   264â†’
   265â†’- NFR15: Dashboard remains functional (displays last known data) when all external APIs are unreachable
   266â†’- NFR16: No JavaScript errors or white screens on WebSocket disconnection
   267â†’- NFR17: Browser tab remains stable during extended sessions (no memory leaks over 24+ hours)
   268â†’

# UX Design Specification - Metrics

**Author:** Dmytro
**Date:** 2026-03-25

---

## Executive Summary

### Project Vision

Single-page, real-time Bitcoin dashboard designed around three UX pillars: speed (information available in < 1 second of glancing), aesthetics (dark crypto visual identity that feels premium and alive), and focus (one asset, one page, zero cognitive overhead). The product competes on depth of simplicity â€” every design decision serves the "open tab, know the price, move on" experience.

### Target Users

Primary user: Dmytro, a developer who uses Metrics as a pinned browser tab for daily BTC price checking. Desktop-first with occasional mobile glances. Expects zero configuration, zero onboarding, and instant information delivery. Tech-savvy but values beauty and polish â€” the dashboard should feel crafted, not cobbled.

### Key Design Challenges

- **Information hierarchy:** Four data elements (price, 24h change, market cap, chart) on a single screen require clear visual priority. Live price dominates; supporting data is secondary but instantly parseable.
- **Dark theme readability:** Near-black backgrounds (#0a0a0f) with neon accents demand careful contrast management. Price figures must be high-contrast without causing eye strain during extended pinned-tab viewing.
- **State transitions:** WebSocket connection states (connecting, live, reconnecting, stale) must be communicated subtly without disrupting the primary price display. No layout shift during any state change.

### Design Opportunities

- **Sub-second glanceability:** Nail the information hierarchy so the price registers in peripheral vision. Tab title with live price extends this to no-focus-needed awareness.
- **Motion as polish:** Entry animations, price tick effects, and chart drawing create a sense of liveness and premium feel. Motion communicates "this is real-time" without needing a label.
- **Dark crypto aesthetic as identity:** Glassmorphism cards, neon accents, and monospaced price typography create a distinctive visual brand that's portfolio-worthy and instantly recognizable.

## Core User Experience

### Defining Experience

The core interaction is **the glance** â€” a sub-second visual scan that delivers complete BTC awareness. No clicks, no scrolling, no reading. The price registers in peripheral vision from a pinned tab; supporting data (change, cap, trend) is absorbed in a single focused look. The product's entire value is delivered passively. The user invests zero effort and receives complete information.

### Platform Strategy

- **Primary platform:** Desktop web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) as a pinned tab
- **Secondary platform:** Mobile web browser for on-the-go checks
- **Input model:** Read-only â€” no mouse interaction required for core experience. Hover tooltips on chart are the only interactive element.
- **Offline consideration:** Not applicable â€” the product's value is real-time data. When offline, display last known data with stale indicator.
- **Tab title:** Leveraged as a zero-focus information channel â€” price visible without switching to the tab

### Effortless Interactions

Everything that should happen without user intervention:

- **Price updates:** WebSocket stream delivers ticks automatically â€” no refresh, no polling button
- **Data refresh:** Market cap and 24h stats refresh via REST every 60s â€” invisible to user
- **Reconnection:** WebSocket drops are handled silently with exponential backoff. User sees a subtle indicator only if reconnection takes more than a few seconds.
- **Theme rendering:** Dark theme is the default and only theme â€” renders immediately with no flash of white
- **Initial hydration:** Static shell appears instantly; data fades in smoothly as WebSocket connects. No loading spinners, no skeleton screens.
- **Responsive adaptation:** Layout shifts from horizontal (desktop) to stacked (mobile) based on viewport â€” no user toggle

### Critical Success Moments

1. **The First Load (make-or-break):** Dark background fills the screen. Static shell renders. Price fades in with animation. Chart draws itself. Under 1.5 seconds from URL to live data. If this moment feels slow, broken, or visually jarring â€” the product fails its promise.
2. **The Daily Glance (retention):** User switches to pinned tab and price is already there, already current, already beautiful. This moment must be 100% reliable. Any stale data, broken layout, or disconnection erodes trust.
3. **The Reconnect (resilience):** WiFi drops, WebSocket disconnects. The transition from "live" to "reconnecting" to "live again" must be smooth and nearly invisible. The user should barely notice it happened.

### Experience Principles

1. **Passive over active:** The user should never have to do anything. Information is delivered, not retrieved.
2. **Instant over progressive:** Show the answer immediately. No loading states, no progressive disclosure, no "please wait." Static shell + data fade-in is the only acceptable load pattern.
3. **Subtle over loud:** State changes (reconnecting, stale data) are communicated through understated visual cues â€” never modals, toasts, or attention-grabbing alerts. The price display is always the loudest element.
4. **Alive over static:** Gentle motion (price ticks, chart animation, fade-ins) communicates that the data is live and the system is working. Stillness should feel intentional, not broken.

## Desired Emotional Response

### Primary Emotional Goals

- **Calm confidence:** "I know the price." Zero anxiety, zero effort. The information is just there, reliable and current. The feeling of a tool that works so well it disappears.
- **Pride of ownership:** "I built this, it's beautiful, it's mine." The dashboard isn't just functional â€” it's a personal artifact. The visual polish reflects the care that went into building it. Every glance reinforces "I made something good."
- **Quiet control:** The sense of having exactly the information you need, presented exactly how you want it. No one else's ads, no one else's layout decisions, no one else's priorities. This is your window into BTC.

### Emotional Journey Mapping

| Moment | Desired Emotion | Design Lever |
|---|---|---|
| First load | Delight + pride â€” "This looks incredible" | Dark theme fills screen, data animates in smoothly, immediate visual impact |
| Daily glance | Calm confidence â€” "I know" | Price is instantly visible, data is current, no surprises |
| Price moving fast | Engaged awareness â€” "I can see it happening" | Live ticking price creates a sense of connection to the market |
| WiFi drops | Unbothered trust â€” "It'll handle it" | Subtle reconnecting indicator, no alarm, no disruption |
| Showing someone | Pride â€” "Look what I built" | The aesthetic sells itself. Glassmorphism, neon, monospace â€” it looks impressive |
| Mobile check | Same confidence, smaller screen â€” "Still works perfectly" | Responsive layout preserves the feeling, not just the function |

### Micro-Emotions

**Cultivate:**
- **Confidence** over confusion â€” the data hierarchy is instantly clear, never ambiguous
- **Trust** over skepticism â€” live indicators and smooth reconnection prove the data is real and current
- **Delight** over mere satisfaction â€” the animations, typography, and visual details reward attention
- **Ownership** over generic utility â€” this feels personal, not like a commodity widget

**Prevent:**
- **Anxiety** â€” never show alarming error states. Connection issues are handled quietly.
- **Doubt** â€” never leave the user wondering if the price is current. Stale data is explicitly marked.
- **Boredom** â€” the subtle motion (price ticks, chart updates) keeps the display feeling alive without being distracting

### Design Implications

- **Pride of craft â†’ Visual polish is non-negotiable.** Every pixel matters. Glassmorphism blur must be smooth, typography spacing must be precise, animations must be 60 FPS. This is a portfolio piece â€” it should look like one.
- **Calm confidence â†’ Information hierarchy is the #1 UX priority.** The price must be the loudest element by far. Supporting data is clearly secondary. No visual competition.
- **Trust â†’ State communication must be honest but calm.** "Reconnecting..." in muted text â€” never a red error banner. Stale data badge â€” never a blocking modal. The system tells the truth without raising its voice.
- **Ownership â†’ No generic UI patterns.** The dark crypto aesthetic is the product's personality. It should feel bespoke, not template-driven. Custom color palette, intentional typography pairing, considered spacing.

### Emotional Design Principles

1. **Polish earns pride.** Every detail that's refined â€” a smooth animation, a perfect font size, a considered color choice â€” compounds into the feeling of "I made something good." Cut no corners on visual quality.
2. **Reliability earns trust.** The data is always there, always current, always correct. When it's not, the system says so honestly and fixes itself quietly. Trust is built through consistent, boring reliability.
3. **Restraint earns calm.** The product never demands attention. It never interrupts. It never shows more than needed. Calm comes from the absence of noise, not the presence of calming elements.
4. **Personality earns ownership.** The neon-on-dark aesthetic, the monospaced prices, the glassmorphism cards â€” these aren't just style choices, they're identity. The product should feel like *yours*, not like a generic dashboard with a dark mode toggle.

## UX Pattern Analysis & Inspiration

### Inspiring Products Analysis

**1. TradingView (chart widget, not the full platform)**

What they nail: The lightweight-charts library (which we're using) comes from TradingView. Their embedded chart widgets are a masterclass in information density on dark backgrounds. Price is always the hero element. The chart feels alive â€” crosshair tracking, smooth transitions between timeframes, and a color system that makes green/red changes instantly parseable. Their dark theme is the industry standard that every crypto dashboard references.

**Lesson for Metrics:** The chart interaction model (hover crosshair with price/time tooltip) is proven and familiar. Don't reinvent it â€” adopt it directly from lightweight-charts defaults.

**2. Is It Down Right Now (isitdownrightnow.com) / Downdetector**

What they nail: Single-purpose, single-question tools. You go there with one question ("Is X down?"), you get the answer immediately, you leave. Zero navigation, zero onboarding, zero accounts. The UX is the answer. This is the closest interaction model to what Metrics does â€” except Metrics does it with visual polish.

**Lesson for Metrics:** The "answer is the page" pattern. No hero section, no welcome message, no instructions. The data IS the interface. Load â†’ answer â†’ done.

**3. Stripe Dashboard (dark mode)**

What they nail: Premium dark UI with clear information hierarchy. Numbers are the loudest elements â€” large, monospaced, high-contrast white on near-black. Supporting text is muted gray. Cards with subtle borders and shadows create visual grouping without cluttering. State indicators (live, test mode) are minimal colored badges. The whole thing feels expensive and considered.

**Lesson for Metrics:** The hierarchy pattern â€” large monospaced numbers dominate, labels are quiet, cards group without competing. And the way Stripe handles state badges (small, colored, unobtrusive) maps directly to our reconnecting/stale data indicators.

### Transferable UX Patterns

**Information Hierarchy:**
- Large monospaced price as the dominant visual element (TradingView, Stripe)
- Muted secondary labels that don't compete with data (Stripe)
- Color-coded directional indicators â€” green up, red down â€” no labels needed (TradingView)

**Data Liveness:**
- Price tick animation on update â€” brief color flash or number transition (TradingView)
- Chart crosshair with tooltip on hover for detail-on-demand (TradingView lightweight-charts)
- No explicit "last updated" timestamp for live data â€” liveness is communicated through motion, not text

**Single-Purpose Layout:**
- Data IS the interface â€” no navigation, no sidebar, no header chrome (Is It Down)
- Answer visible within 0 scrolls â€” everything above the fold on all devices (Is It Down)
- The page is the product â€” no routes, no states, no modes

**Dark Theme Execution:**
- Near-black backgrounds with subtle card elevation via border or shadow, not background contrast (Stripe)
- Glassmorphism for primary data cards, solid backgrounds for secondary elements
- Neon accents used sparingly â€” for interactive elements and status indicators, not decoration

### Anti-Patterns to Avoid

- **CoinGecko/CoinMarketCap:** Dozens of coins, ads, nav bars, footer links, trending lists, news feeds. The opposite of focus. Everything competes for attention.
- **Loading spinners:** Products that show a spinner before data appears create anxiety. Metrics should show the static shell immediately and fade data in â€” never a blank screen with a spinner.
- **"Last updated: 3 seconds ago" timestamps:** These create doubt ("is 3 seconds old?"). Live motion (ticking price) communicates freshness better than any timestamp.
- **Toast notifications for connection events:** "Connected!" / "Disconnected!" toasts are noisy and interruptive. State should be communicated through subtle, persistent indicators â€” not ephemeral popups.
- **Skeleton screens for simple data:** Skeleton loaders are for complex layouts with unknown content. For four known data elements, reserved space with a quick fade-in is cleaner and faster-feeling.

### Design Inspiration Strategy

**Adopt directly:**
- lightweight-charts hover crosshair with price/time tooltip (proven, familiar)
- Large monospaced price as visual anchor (TradingView/Stripe pattern)
- Green/red color coding for price direction (universal convention)
- "Answer is the page" single-screen layout (Is It Down pattern)

**Adapt for Metrics:**
- Stripe's card hierarchy â†’ glassmorphism cards with neon accent borders instead of subtle gray borders
- Stripe's state badges â†’ minimal connection status indicator (colored dot + text, not a banner)
- TradingView's price tick animation â†’ brief color flash on price change (cyan flash up, red flash down) that fades to white

**Avoid:**
- Any navigation elements, sidebars, or header chrome
- Loading spinners or skeleton screens
- Toast notifications for system events
- Explicit "last updated" timestamps for live data
- Any multi-page patterns or routing

## Design System Foundation

### Design System Choice

**Tailwind CSS utility framework** with a custom design token layer. No component library â€” the product has only 4-5 visual elements (price display, change indicator, market cap, chart card, status indicator), making a full component library unnecessary overhead. Tailwind provides the utility backbone; custom tokens define the crypto aesthetic.

### Rationale for Selection

- **Visual uniqueness required:** The dark crypto aesthetic (glassmorphism, neon accents, monospace prices) is too specific for any off-the-shelf component library. Custom styling is mandatory.
- **Minimal component surface:** A single page with ~5 visual elements doesn't justify the bundle cost or learning curve of MUI, Chakra, or Ant Design.
- **Already specified in PRD:** Tailwind CSS was chosen during product planning â€” this decision maintains consistency.
- **Solo developer efficiency:** Tailwind's utility classes allow rapid iteration without context-switching between CSS files and components. Custom tokens ensure consistency without a design system overhead.
- **Bundle performance:** Tailwind's purge removes unused styles. No component library JS to ship. Aligns with the < 100KB gzipped JS budget.

### Implementation Approach

**Tailwind Config as Design System:**

The `tailwind.config.js` file serves as the single source of truth for all design tokens, using `theme.extend` (not override) to preserve Tailwind defaults:

- **Colors (semantic naming):** `accent-primary` (cyan), `accent-secondary` (purple), `accent-tertiary` (green), `price-up`, `price-down`, `bg-base`, `bg-card`, `text-primary`, `text-muted`, `text-label` â€” never raw color names like `cyan-400`
- **Typography:** JetBrains Mono for price figures, Inter for labels â€” configured as named font families (`font-price`, `font-label`) with appropriate size/weight scales
- **Effects:** Glassmorphism utilities (backdrop-blur, border opacity), glow effects for neon accents, transition presets for animations
- **Spacing:** Custom spacing scale if Tailwind defaults don't match the desired density

**CSS Custom Properties from Day One:**

CSS variables alongside Tailwind tokens for any value that animates or transitions at runtime:
- `--price-flash-up` / `--price-flash-down` â€” for price tick color flash animations (can't transition between Tailwind utility classes)
- `--connection-status` â€” for status indicator color transitions
- Defined in `:root` and synchronized with Tailwind token values

**Shared Constants File:**

A `theme-constants.ts` file that exports color values used by both Tailwind config and lightweight-charts API config. The chart library applies its own inline styles (line color, crosshair, grid lines) via its JS API â€” these must mirror the Tailwind tokens but can't reference them directly. A shared constants file prevents drift.

### Customization Strategy

- **Semantic token naming** throughout â€” tokens communicate intent (`accent-primary`, `text-muted`) not implementation (`cyan-400`, `gray-500`). If the accent color changes from cyan to green, no token names read wrong.
- **Reusable utility compositions** via `@apply` for 3 repeated patterns maximum: `.card-glass` (glassmorphism card), `.price-text` (large monospaced price styling), `.status-badge` (connection state indicator). No more â€” overusing `@apply` defeats utility-first and complicates debugging.
- **Astro scoped styles** for animation keyframes co-located with each island component, while Tailwind handles layout and token-based styling
- **No component abstraction layer** for v1 â€” with only ~5 visual elements, inline Tailwind classes are sufficient. Component extraction happens in Phase 3 (reusable library) if needed.

## Defining Experience

### The Core Interaction

**"Glance at a tab and know the Bitcoin price."**

The defining experience is passive information absorption. The user does nothing â€” the product delivers. Like a wall clock on the wall: you don't operate it, you don't configure it, you just look at it and you know. If Metrics nails this one interaction â€” instant, reliable, beautiful awareness â€” everything else follows.

### User Mental Model

**Current mental model (what Metrics replaces):**
- "I need to check the Bitcoin price" â†’ open Google/CoinGecko â†’ scan through noise â†’ find the number â†’ close or navigate away
- Active retrieval: go somewhere, look something up, extract the answer, leave

**New mental model (what Metrics creates):**
- "I wonder what Bitcoin is at" â†’ glance at pinned tab â†’ know
- Passive awareness: the answer is already there, always current, always visible
- Analogous to a wall clock â€” you don't "use" it, you just look at it

**Mental model shift:** From "go find information" to "information is already here." This is the core UX insight. The product doesn't feel like a tool you operate â€” it feels like a window you look through.

### Success Criteria for Core Experience

- **Sub-second comprehension:** The price registers within one visual scan â€” no reading, no parsing, no searching the page
- **Zero-action awareness:** The user performs no clicks, no scrolls, no hovers to get the primary information
- **Persistent reliability:** Every time the user glances at the tab, the data is there and current. 100% consistency builds the "wall clock" trust
- **Tab-level awareness:** The browser tab title shows the price â€” awareness without even switching to the tab
- **Beautiful by default:** The visual quality reinforces "I built something good" on every glance

### Pattern Analysis

**Established patterns only â€” no novel interaction design required.**

All interaction patterns in Metrics are proven and familiar:
- Real-time dashboard display (TradingView, Stripe, any analytics tool)
- Sparkline chart with hover tooltip (universal data visualization pattern)
- Color-coded up/down indicators (universal financial convention)
- Connection status indicators (any real-time app)
- Dark theme dashboard (industry standard for financial/crypto tools)

**The innovation is curation, not interaction.** Metrics takes established patterns and applies radical subtraction â€” removing everything that isn't the answer. No new patterns to teach, no learning curve, no onboarding.

### Experience Mechanics

**1. Initiation:**
- User opens browser â†’ pinned tab is already there (no action required)
- OR user navigates to URL â†’ page loads in < 1.5 seconds
- No splash screen, no welcome, no onboarding â€” the data IS the landing page

**2. Interaction (passive):**
- **Primary:** Eyes land on the page â†’ large monospaced price is immediately visible â†’ comprehension complete
- **Secondary (optional):** Eyes scan downward â†’ 24h change, market cap, sparkline chart absorbed in a single focused look
- **Detail-on-demand:** Hover over chart â†’ crosshair with price/time tooltip for specific data points (only interactive element)

**3. Feedback:**
- **Liveness:** Price ticks with brief color flash (cyan up, red down) â€” confirms data is live without any label
- **Direction:** Green/red color on 24h change â€” instant positive/negative signal
- **Trend:** Sparkline chart shape â€” upward, downward, flat trend readable at a glance
- **Connection:** Subtle status indicator â€” only visible when something is wrong (reconnecting, stale)

**4. Completion:**
- There is no completion. The experience is continuous. The user glances, absorbs, and moves on. The tab stays open. The data keeps updating. The next glance will be just as effortless.

## Visual Design Foundation

### Color System

**Background Palette:**
- `bg-base`: #0a0a0f â€” near-black with blue undertone, primary page background
- `bg-card`: #12121a â€” slightly elevated, for glassmorphism card backgrounds
- `bg-card-hover`: #1a1a2e â€” subtle hover/active state for interactive elements

**Accent Palette (semantic naming):**
- `accent-primary`: #00f5ff (cyan) â€” primary interactive accent, chart line color, active states
- `accent-secondary`: #8b5cf6 (electric purple) â€” secondary accent, used sparingly for visual interest (card borders, glow effects)
- `accent-glow`: rgba(0, 245, 255, 0.15) â€” subtle cyan glow for card borders and focus states

**Semantic Colors:**
- `price-up`: #22c55e (green) â€” positive price change, upward indicators
- `price-down`: #ef4444 (red) â€” negative price change, downward indicators
- `price-flash-up`: #00f5ff (cyan flash) â€” brief flash on price tick up, fades to white
- `price-flash-down`: #ef4444 (red flash) â€” brief flash on price tick down, fades to white

**Text Palette:**
- `text-primary`: #f0f0f0 â€” high-contrast white for price figures and primary data
- `text-secondary`: #a0a0b0 â€” muted for labels ("24h Change", "Market Cap")
- `text-muted`: #606070 â€” subtle for meta-information and status text ("Reconnecting...")

**Status Colors:**
- `status-live`: #22c55e â€” small green dot when WebSocket is connected
- `status-reconnecting`: #f59e0b (amber) â€” reconnection in progress
- `status-stale`: #ef4444 â€” data may be outdated

**Contrast Ratios (WCAG targets):**
- `text-primary` on `bg-base`: 15.2:1 (AAA)
- `text-secondary` on `bg-base`: 6.8:1 (AA)
- `text-muted` on `bg-base`: 3.5:1 (meets minimum for large text)
- `price-up` on `bg-card`: 5.2:1 (AA)
- `price-down` on `bg-card`: 4.6:1 (AA)

### Typography System

**Font Families:**
- `font-price`: JetBrains Mono â€” monospaced, used for all numerical data (price, change, market cap). Monospace ensures digits don't shift width on update, preventing layout jitter.
- `font-label`: Inter â€” geometric sans-serif, used for all labels, status text, and metadata. Clean, neutral, highly legible at small sizes on screens.

**Type Scale:**

| Element | Font | Size | Weight | Color |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Live price | JetBrains Mono | 48px (desktop) / 36px (mobile) | 600 | `text-primary` |
| 24h change value | JetBrains Mono | 20px | 500 | `price-up` or `price-down` |
| Market cap value | JetBrains Mono | 20px | 500 | `text-primary` |
| Card labels | Inter | 12px | 400 | `text-secondary` |
| Status text | Inter | 11px | 400 | `text-muted` |
| Chart tooltip | JetBrains Mono | 13px | 400 | `text-primary` |

**Line Heights:**
- Price figures: 1.1 (tight, monospaced numbers don't need extra leading)
- Labels: 1.4 (comfortable reading)
- Status text: 1.3

### Spacing & Layout Foundation

**Spacing Scale (8px base):**
- `xs`: 4px â€” inner padding for small elements (status badge)
- `sm`: 8px â€” gap between label and value within a card
- `md`: 16px â€” padding inside cards
- `lg`: 24px â€” gap between cards
- `xl`: 32px â€” page margin / major section spacing

**Desktop Layout:**
- Centered content container, max-width 800px
- Single row of glassmorphism cards: [Price + Change] [Market Cap] [Chart]
- Price card is visually dominant (~40% of width)
- Chart card spans full width below, or sits as the largest card in the row
- Vertical centering on viewport â€” content sits in the visual center, not top-anchored

**Mobile Layout (< 640px):**
- Full-width stacked cards with `lg` (24px) gaps
- Price at top (largest), change below, market cap, then chart fills remaining width
- `md` (16px) horizontal page padding

**Card Styling (glassmorphism):**
- Background: `bg-card` with `backdrop-blur(12px)`
- Border: 1px solid `rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.08)` â€” barely visible edge definition
- Border-radius: 16px
- Optional: subtle `accent-secondary` glow on border (`box-shadow: 0 0 20px rgba(139, 92, 246, 0.1)`)
- No drop shadows on dark backgrounds â€” they disappear. Use border glow instead.

### Accessibility Considerations

- All text meets AA contrast minimum against its background
- Price figures (largest text) exceed AAA at 15.2:1
- Green/red price indicators are supplemented by directional arrows (â†‘/â†“) â€” not color-only
- Monospaced font prevents layout shift on number changes â€” reduces cognitive load
- No flashing content faster than 3 times per second (price flash animation is a single brief transition)
- Semantic HTML ensures screen readers can access all data values

## Design Direction Decision

### Design Directions Explored

Six layout directions were generated and evaluated via interactive HTML showcase (`ux-design-directions.html`):

- **A: Hero Price** â€” Centered vertical stack, price dominates
- **B: Card Grid** â€” Structured glassmorphism cards in 2-column grid
- **C: Compact Strip** â€” Dense horizontal info bar + large chart
- **D: Immersive** â€” Full-screen chart background with data overlay
- **E: Minimal** â€” Giant typography, whisper-quiet supporting data
- **F: Asymmetric** â€” Price-heavy left, chart right

### Chosen Direction

**Direction B: Card Grid**

Two-column grid layout with glassmorphism cards:
- **Price card** (left, ~60% width): Live price at 48px, 24h change below (percentage + absolute)
- **Market cap card** (right, ~40% width): Market cap value with 24h range
- **Chart card** (full width, below): 24h sparkline chart spanning both columns
- **Status indicator**: Small "Live" badge with green dot, bottom of viewport

The grid structure creates natural visual grouping â€” each data element lives in its own card, making the hierarchy instantly clear. Price card is visually dominant through size. The full-width chart card gives the sparkline maximum horizontal space for readable trend shape.

### Design Rationale

- **Structured hierarchy:** Cards create clear visual boundaries between data elements. The eye knows where to look for each piece of information.
- **Glassmorphism showcase:** Multiple cards give more surface area for the glassmorphism effect â€” card borders, subtle purple glow, backdrop blur. The aesthetic has room to breathe.
- **Scalable foundation:** If Phase 2 adds more data (extended timeframes, additional metrics), new cards slot naturally into the grid without redesigning the layout.
- **Desktop-to-mobile mapping:** The 2-column grid collapses to a single-column stack on mobile with no information loss â€” each card remains self-contained.
- **Pride of craft:** The card grid looks like a designed product, not a quick hack. Multiple refined cards compound the "I built something good" feeling.

## User Journey Flows

### Flow 1: Initial Load

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> URLEntered: User navigates to Metrics
    URLEntered --> StaticShell: Astro serves static HTML
    StaticShell --> DarkBackground: bg-base renders immediately (no FOUC)
    DarkBackground --> CardSkeletons: Empty glassmorphism cards visible with labels
    CardSkeletons --> IslandHydration: Astro hydrates client islands
    IslandHydration --> WSConnect: WebSocket connects to Binance
    IslandHydration --> RESTFetch: CoinGecko REST call (market cap, 24h data)
    WSConnect --> WSSuccess: Connection established
    WSConnect --> WSFail: Binance unavailable
    WSFail --> CoinCapFallback: Try CoinCap WebSocket
    CoinCapFallback --> WSSuccess: Fallback connected
    CoinCapFallback --> RESTPolling: Both WebSockets failed
    RESTPolling --> DataReady: REST polling active (60s interval)
    WSSuccess --> DataReady: First price tick received
    RESTFetch --> DataReady: Market cap + 24h data loaded
    DataReady --> FadeIn: Price, change, cap fade in with animation
    FadeIn --> ChartDraw: Sparkline draws with animation
    ChartDraw --> LiveState: Dashboard fully operational
    LiveState --> TabTitle: Tab title updates with price
    LiveState --> [*]
```

**Timing targets:**
- Static shell visible: < 200ms
- Islands hydrated: < 800ms
- First data visible: < 1.5s
- Fully operational: < 2s

**UX detail:** Empty cards render with labels ("Bitcoin BTC/USD", "Market Cap", "24h Chart") but no data values. This avoids layout shift â€” the space is reserved. Data fades in with a 300ms ease-out transition. Chart draws left-to-right with a 500ms animation.

### Flow 2: Live Operation (Steady State)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle: Dashboard is live, user is elsewhere
    Idle --> TabGlance: User sees price in tab title
    TabGlance --> Aware: User knows price without switching
    TabGlance --> FocusTab: User switches to Metrics tab
    Idle --> FocusTab: User switches to Metrics tab
    FocusTab --> PriceRead: Eyes land on price card (largest element)
    PriceRead --> ChangeRead: Eyes scan to 24h change (green/red)
    ChangeRead --> ChartScan: Eyes drop to sparkline (trend shape)
    ChartScan --> Done: User has complete picture
    PriceRead --> Done: User only needed the price
    Done --> Idle: User switches away

    state "Background Activity" as bg {
        WSStream --> PriceTick: New trade received
        PriceTick --> FlashColor: Brief cyan (up) or red (down) flash
        FlashColor --> UpdateDOM: Price text updates
        UpdateDOM --> UpdateTab: Tab title updates
        UpdateTab --> WSStream: Wait for next tick
        RESTTimer --> RESTCall: Every 60 seconds
        RESTCall --> UpdateMcap: Market cap refreshes
        UpdateMcap --> Update24h: 24h change refreshes
        Update24h --> RESTTimer: Reset timer
    }
```

**UX detail:** Price updates are near-continuous (Binance sends tick-by-tick trades). Each update triggers a brief color flash â€” the price text transitions to `price-flash-up` (cyan) or `price-flash-down` (red) then fades back to `text-primary` (white) over 400ms. This creates the "alive" feeling without being distracting. Market cap and 24h change update silently every 60s â€” no flash needed for these.

### Flow 3: Connection Recovery

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Live: Dashboard operating normally
    Live --> Disconnect: WebSocket connection drops
    Disconnect --> ShowIndicator: "Reconnecting..." appears in text-muted
    ShowIndicator --> Attempt1: Backoff: 1 second
    Attempt1 --> Reconnected: Success
    Attempt1 --> Attempt2: Fail â†’ backoff: 2 seconds
    Attempt2 --> Reconnected: Success
    Attempt2 --> Attempt3: Fail â†’ backoff: 4 seconds
    Attempt3 --> Reconnected: Success
    Attempt3 --> AttemptN: Fail â†’ continue doubling (max 30s)
    AttemptN --> Reconnected: Success
    AttemptN --> StaleState: Extended failure (> 30 seconds)
    StaleState --> ShowStale: Stale data badge appears on price card
    StaleState --> TryFallback: Switch to CoinCap / REST polling
    TryFallback --> FallbackLive: Fallback connected
    FallbackLive --> HideStale: Remove stale badge, show "Live" again
    TryFallback --> StaleState: All sources failed
    Reconnected --> HideIndicator: Remove "Reconnecting..." text
    HideIndicator --> Live: Resume normal operation
    HideStale --> Live: Resume normal operation

    Live --> Expiry24h: Binance 24h connection expiry
    Expiry24h --> Disconnect: Treated as normal disconnect
```

**UX detail:** The "Reconnecting..." text appears in the status area (bottom of viewport) using `text-muted` color â€” barely visible, never alarming. If reconnection takes > 30 seconds, a small badge appears on the price card: "Stale" in `status-stale` color. The price value itself stays visible (last known value) â€” never blank, never hidden. When connection restores, the indicator fades out over 300ms and the next price tick triggers a flash as usual â€” confirming liveness.

### Flow 4: Responsive Adaptation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DetectViewport: Page loads or viewport resizes
    DetectViewport --> Desktop: Width â‰¥ 640px
    DetectViewport --> Mobile: Width < 640px

    Desktop --> TwoColGrid: grid-template-columns 1.5fr 1fr
    TwoColGrid --> PriceLeft: Price card (48px price, change below)
    TwoColGrid --> McapRight: Market cap card (right column)
    TwoColGrid --> ChartFull: Chart card spans both columns
    TwoColGrid --> CenterVert: Container centered vertically on viewport

    Mobile --> SingleCol: Single column, full-width cards
    SingleCol --> PriceTop: Price card (36px price, change below)
    SingleCol --> McapMiddle: Market cap card
    SingleCol --> ChartBottom: Chart card (full width)
    SingleCol --> PadHoriz: 16px horizontal padding, 24px card gaps
```

**UX detail:** The transition between layouts uses CSS Grid + media query â€” no JavaScript. On resize, cards reflow instantly. The price font scales from 48px (desktop) to 36px (mobile) via responsive utility. Chart card adjusts height automatically. All functionality is identical across viewports â€” no features hidden on mobile.

### Journey Patterns

**State Communication Pattern:**
All system states (live, reconnecting, stale) are communicated through a single status area at the bottom of the viewport. States escalate visually: live (green dot, nearly invisible) â†’ reconnecting (muted text, subtle) â†’ stale (colored badge on price card, more visible). The price display is never disrupted.

**Data Update Pattern:**
Two update cadences coexist: high-frequency (WebSocket price ticks, continuous) and low-frequency (REST market data, every 60s). High-frequency updates get visual feedback (color flash). Low-frequency updates are silent. This creates a natural "heartbeat" â€” the price ticking confirms the system is alive.

**Graceful Degradation Pattern:**
The system degrades through three tiers: WebSocket (primary, real-time) â†’ WebSocket fallback (CoinCap, real-time) â†’ REST polling (60s intervals). Each tier provides the same data at lower fidelity. The UI adapts by adding/removing state indicators but never changes layout or hides data.

### Flow Optimization Principles

1. **Zero-step value delivery:** The user performs zero actions to receive the primary value (current BTC price). Value is delivered on page load, not after interaction.
2. **Escalating visibility for problems:** Good state is invisible (green dot). Problems become visible proportional to their severity. No state demands full attention.
3. **Never blank, never broken:** Every card always shows something â€” either live data or last-known data with a stale indicator. The layout never collapses, shifts, or shows empty states.
4. **Motion confirms life:** The ticking price and color flashes are not decoration â€” they're functional feedback confirming the data pipeline is working.

## Component Strategy

### Design System Components

**Available from Tailwind CSS (utility-only, no pre-built components):**
- Layout utilities (flexbox, grid, spacing, responsive breakpoints)
- Color utilities (mapped to custom semantic tokens)
- Typography utilities (mapped to custom font families and scale)
- Transition and animation utilities
- Backdrop-filter for glassmorphism

**No pre-built UI components** â€” all 5 components are custom. This is intentional: the product has too few elements to justify a component library, and the aesthetic requires full visual control.

### Custom Components

#### 1. Price Card

**Purpose:** Display the live BTC/USD price and 24h change â€” the primary data element and visual anchor of the entire dashboard.

**Anatomy:**
- Card container (`.card-glass` â€” glassmorphism)
- Label: "Bitcoin BTC/USD" (`font-label`, `text-secondary`, 12px)
- Price value: "$68,432.17" (`font-price`, `text-primary`, 48px desktop / 36px mobile, weight 600)
- Change row: "â†‘ +2.3%" + "+$1,534.22" (`font-price`, `price-up` or `price-down`, 16px)

**States:**
- **Live (default):** Price updates with color flash on each tick. Cyan flash (up) or red flash (down) fades to white over 400ms.
- **Stale:** Small badge appears below change row â€” "Stale" in `status-stale` color. Price value remains visible (last known).
- **Loading (initial):** Label visible, price value area reserved but empty. Data fades in with 300ms ease-out on first tick.

**Accessibility:** `<section aria-label="Bitcoin price">`. Price value in `<output>` element for live region announcement. Arrow indicator (â†‘/â†“) supplements color for direction.

#### 2. Market Cap Card

**Purpose:** Display supplementary market data â€” market capitalization and 24h price range.

**Anatomy:**
- Card container (`.card-glass`)
- Label: "Market Cap" (`font-label`, `text-secondary`, 12px)
- Value: "$1.34T" (`font-price`, `text-primary`, 24px, weight 500)
- Secondary label: "24h Range" (`font-label`, `text-secondary`, 12px)
- Range value: "$66,898 â€” $68,890" (`font-price`, `text-primary`, 14px)

- Range value: "$66,898 â€” $68,890" (`font-price`, `text-primary`, 14px) **States:** - **Live (default):** Values update silently every 60s (no flash â€” low-frequency data). - **Loading (initial):** Labels visible, values empty. Data fades in on first REST response. **Accessibility:** `
`. Values in semantic elements. #### 3. Chart Card **Purpose:** Display 24h price trend as an interactive sparkline chart. **Anatomy:** - Card container (`.card-glass`, `grid-column: 1 / -1` on desktop) - Label: "24h Chart" (`font-label`, `text-secondary`, 12px) - Chart canvas: lightweight-charts instance, fills card width - Crosshair tooltip (on hover): price + time (`font-price`, 13px) **States:** - **Live (default):** Chart line in `accent-primary` (cyan). Area fill with subtle cyan gradient. Crosshair appears on hover with price/time tooltip. - **Loading (initial):** Empty chart area. Line draws left-to-right with 500ms animation on first data load. - **Stale:** Chart remains visible with last known data. No visual change on chart itself (stale state shown on Price Card). **Configuration (lightweight-charts API):** - Line color: `accent-primary` (#00f5ff) - Area top color: rgba(0, 245, 255, 0.1) - Area bottom color: transparent - Grid lines: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.03) - Crosshair color: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.3) - Text color: `text-secondary` - Background: transparent (card provides background) - All colors sourced from `theme-constants.ts` **Accessibility:** `
`. Chart is decorative for screen readers â€” price data is available in Price Card. Hover tooltip is enhancement, not required for comprehension. #### 4. Status Indicator **Purpose:** Communicate WebSocket connection state without disrupting the primary data display. **Anatomy:** - Container: centered at bottom of viewport, small and unobtrusive - Dot: 6px circle, color indicates state - Text: state label (`font-label`, `text-muted`, 11px) **States:** - **Live:** Green dot (`status-live`) + "Live" text. Barely visible â€” the good state is quiet. - **Reconnecting:** Amber dot (`status-reconnecting`) + "Reconnecting..." text. Dot pulses with subtle animation. - **Stale:** Red dot (`status-stale`) + "Connection lost" text. Static, no animation. - **Fallback:** Green dot + "Live â€” CoinCap" text. Indicates fallback source is active. **Transitions:** State changes fade over 300ms. No abrupt switches. **Accessibility:** `
`. Screen readers announce state changes. #### 5. Page Shell **Purpose:** Provide the viewport container, background, and layout grid for all cards. **Anatomy:** - Full-viewport background: `bg-base` (#0a0a0f) - Content container: max-width 720px, centered horizontally, centered vertically on viewport - CSS Grid: `grid-template-columns: 1.5fr 1fr` (desktop), single column (mobile) - Gap: 24px between cards - Page padding: 32px (desktop), 16px (mobile) **States:** - **Desktop (â‰¥ 640px):** Two-column grid, vertically centered - **Mobile (< 640px):** Single-column stack, top-aligned with padding **Responsibility:** Sets `` background color to `bg-base` to prevent FOUC. Loads font files. Provides the static shell that renders before islands hydrate. ### Component Implementation Strategy - **All components are Astro components** (`.astro` files) - Price Card and Chart Card are **hydrated client islands** (`client:load`) â€” they manage WebSocket state and DOM updates - Market Cap Card can be a **hydrated island** (manages REST polling) or receive data from a shared service - Status Indicator is a **hydrated island** â€” reacts to connection state changes - Page Shell is **static Astro** â€” no client-side JS needed **Styling approach:** - Inline Tailwind utilities for layout and spacing - `.card-glass` `@apply` composition for shared glassmorphism - `.price-text` `@apply` composition for price figure styling - Astro scoped `

    16â†’---
    17â†’
    18â†’# Architecture Decision Document
    19â†’
    20â†’_This document builds collaboratively through step-by-step discovery. Sections are appended as we work through each architectural decision together._
    21â†’
    22â†’## Project Context Analysis
    23â†’
    24â†’### Requirements Overview
    25â†’
    26â†’**Functional Requirements:**
    27â†’23 FRs across 6 categories. The dominant category is Connection Resilience (FR11â€“FR16) â€” 6 requirements governing WebSocket lifecycle, multi-source fallback, and stale data handling. This is the most architecturally significant area despite the project's low overall complexity. The remaining FRs cover straightforward data display (price, change, market cap, chart) and visual presentation (dark theme, animations, responsive layout).
    28â†’
    29â†’**Non-Functional Requirements:**
    30â†’17 NFRs with specific quantified targets. Performance NFRs (1â€“8) define a tight bundle budget (<100KB JS, <50KB chart lib) and strict rendering metrics (CLS = 0, LCP <1.5s, 60 FPS). Integration NFRs (9â€“14) specify WebSocket connection behavior (500ms connect, exponential backoff 1sâ€“30s, 60s REST polling). Reliability NFRs (15â€“17) require graceful degradation and no memory leaks over 24h+ sessions.
    31â†’
    32â†’**Scale & Complexity:**
    33â†’
    34â†’- Primary domain: Frontend web â€” real-time data visualization
    35â†’- Complexity level: Low
    36â†’- Estimated architectural components: 5 UI components + 1 WebSocket service layer + 1 REST polling service + 1 shared theme constants module
    37â†’
    38â†’### Technical Constraints & Dependencies
    39â†’
    40â†’- **Framework locked:** Astro JS with Islands Architecture â€” non-negotiable
    41â†’- **Styling locked:** Tailwind CSS with custom semantic tokens
    42â†’- **Chart library:** lightweight-charts (TradingView) â€” chosen for finance-native API, ~40KB gzipped
    43â†’- **External API dependencies (no auth required):**
    44â†’  - Binance WebSocket (primary real-time) â€” 24h connection expiry, potential geo-restrictions
    45â†’  - CoinCap WebSocket (fallback real-time) â€” simpler, universal access
    46â†’  - CoinGecko REST (supplementary data) â€” 30 req/min free tier, 60s polling interval
    47â†’- **No backend, no SSR, no routing, no SEO, no user data storage**
    48â†’- **Browser targets:** Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge (latest 2 versions) â€” backdrop-filter supported across all
    49â†’
    50â†’### Cross-Cutting Concerns Identified
    51â†’
    52â†’1. **WebSocket connection state** â€” Shared across Price Card, Status Indicator, and fallback logic. Needs a unified connection manager that multiple islands can observe.
    53â†’2. **Design token consistency** â€” Tailwind config tokens must stay in sync with lightweight-charts JS API colors. Solved via shared `theme-constants.ts` file.
    54â†’3. **Stale data handling** â€” Affects Price Card (badge display), Status Indicator (state text), and tab title (should it show stale price?). Needs a consistent staleness threshold (>30s).
    55â†’4. **Animation/motion standards** â€” Consistent timing (300ms/400ms/500ms), easing curves, and `prefers-reduced-motion` support across all components.
    56â†’5. **Number formatting** â€” USD formatting with comma separators, abbreviated market cap, consistent decimal places across all data displays.
    57â†’
    58â†’## Starter Template Evaluation
    59â†’
    60â†’### Primary Technology Domain
    61â†’
    62â†’Frontend web â€” single-page real-time dashboard built on Astro Islands Architecture.
    63â†’
    64â†’### Starter Options Considered
    65â†’
    66â†’| Option | Template | Fit | Verdict |
    67â†’|---|---|---|---|
    68â†’| A | `create astro -- --template minimal` | Perfect â€” clean slate, zero cruft | **Selected** |
    69â†’| B | Astroplate / AstroWind / Astroship | Poor â€” blog/content-site scaffolding to remove | Rejected |
    70â†’| C | Manual setup | No advantage over CLI wizard | Rejected |
    71â†’
    72â†’### Selected Starter: Astro Minimal + CLI Integrations
    73â†’
    74â†’**Rationale:** The project has exactly 1 page, 5 components, and no content management. Any opinionated starter adds complexity to remove. The minimal template + `astro add` commands give us the exact stack specified in the PRD with zero waste.
    75â†’
    76â†’**Initialization Commands:**
    77â†’
    78â†’```bash
    79â†’npm create astro@latest -- --template minimal --typescript strict
    80â†’npx astro add tailwind
    81â†’npm install lightweight-charts @fontsource/jetbrains-mono @fontsource/inter
    82â†’npm install -D eslint prettier eslint-plugin-astro prettier-plugin-astro
    83â†’npm install -D vitest
    84â†’```
    85â†’
    86â†’**Package manager:** npm (explicit choice â€” simplest for solo developer, no pnpm/yarn complexity needed).
    87â†’
    88â†’### Architectural Decisions Provided by Starter
    89â†’
    90â†’**Language & Runtime:**
    91â†’- Astro 6.x (current stable 6.0.8) â€” requires Node 22+
    92â†’- TypeScript in strict mode â€” catches type errors early, appropriate for intermediate developer
    93â†’- Astro's built-in TypeScript support (no separate tsconfig needed beyond Astro defaults)
    94â†’- Note: Astro 6 is a recent stable release. Headline features (redesigned dev server, CSP APIs, live content collections) aren't needed for this project â€” we're on Astro 6 because `create astro@latest` gives us Astro 6. Acceptable early-adopter risk for a personal project with no user data.
    95â†’
    96â†’**Styling Solution:**
    97â†’- Tailwind CSS v4 via `@tailwindcss/vite` plugin
    98â†’- CSS-first configuration: `@theme` directives in `src/styles/global.css` replace `tailwind.config.js` for token definition
    99â†’- Design tokens (`accent-primary`, `bg-base`, `text-muted`, etc.) defined as CSS custom properties via `@theme`
   100â†’- **Tailwind v4 â†” lightweight-charts token strategy:** Colors needed by the lightweight-charts JS API cannot be read from CSS `@theme` at import time. For v1, duplicate color values in a `theme-constants.ts` file â€” acceptable sync risk for a solo developer with 5 color values. Refactor to runtime `getComputedStyle()` reads if a theme switcher is ever added.
   101â†’
   102â†’**Build Tooling:**
   103â†’- Vite (bundled with Astro) â€” HMR, tree-shaking, optimized production builds
   104â†’- Astro's static output mode (`output: 'static'`) â€” generates pure HTML/CSS/JS files
   105â†’- Automatic CSS purging via Tailwind v4
   106â†’
   107â†’**Code Quality:**
   108â†’- ESLint with `eslint-plugin-astro` for Astro-aware linting
   109â†’- Prettier with `prettier-plugin-astro` for consistent formatting
   110â†’- Configured from first commit â€” important if project becomes open-source in Phase 3
   111â†’
   112â†’**Testing Framework:**
   113â†’- Vitest included in dev dependencies from project init
   114â†’- Recommended test strategy for v1: unit tests for WebSocket manager and number formatting utilities, single Playwright smoke test for "page loads, price appears"
   115â†’- Note: if chart config uses runtime `getComputedStyle()` in future, CSS values won't be available in Node.js test environment â€” the duplicated `theme-constants.ts` approach keeps test imports trivial
   116â†’
   117â†’**Code Organization:**
   118â†’- `src/pages/` â€” single `index.astro` page
   119â†’- `src/components/` â€” Astro components (islands)
   120â†’- `src/layouts/` â€” page layout wrapper
   121â†’- `src/styles/` â€” global CSS with Tailwind `@import` and `@theme` tokens
   122â†’- `src/lib/` â€” shared utilities (`theme-constants.ts`, formatting helpers, WebSocket manager)
   123â†’
   124â†’**Development Experience:**
   125â†’- Astro dev server with HMR
   126â†’- TypeScript type checking
   127â†’- Tailwind CSS IntelliSense (VS Code extension)
   128â†’
   129â†’**Note:** Project initialization using these commands should be the first implementation story.
   130â†’
   131â†’## Core Architectural Decisions
   132â†’
   133â†’### Decision Priority Analysis
   134â†’
   135â†’**Critical Decisions (Block Implementation):**
   136â†’1. Inter-island state management â€” Nano Stores
   137â†’2. Island UI framework â€” Vanilla TypeScript (no framework)
   138â†’3. WebSocket manager architecture â€” single manager, multiple consumers via stores
   139â†’
   140â†’**Important Decisions (Shape Architecture):**
   141â†’4. Message normalization â€” common `PriceTick` interface across data sources
   142â†’5. Stale data threshold â€” 30 seconds
   143â†’
   144â†’**Deferred Decisions (Post-MVP):**
   145â†’- Hosting provider selection (Phase 2)
   146â†’- CI/CD pipeline (Phase 2)
   147â†’- PWA configuration (Phase 2)
   148â†’- Component library extraction (Phase 3)
   149â†’
   150â†’### Data Architecture
   151â†’
   152â†’No database. No persistent storage. All data is ephemeral â€” sourced from external APIs and held in memory via Nano Stores.
   153â†’
   154â†’**Data flow:**
   155â†’```
   156â†’Binance WebSocket â”€â”€â†’ ConnectionManager â”€â”€â†’ Nano Store (priceStore) â”€â”€â†’ Price Card island
   157â†’CoinCap WebSocket â”€â”ک  (fallback)           Nano Store (statusStore) â”€â”€â†’ Status Indicator island
   158â†’                                            Nano Store (priceStore) â”€â”€â†’ Tab title updater
   159â†’
   160â†’CoinGecko REST â”€â”€â”€â”€â†’ MarketDataService â”€â”€â†’ Nano Store (marketStore) â”€â”€â†’ Market Cap Card island
   161â†’                     (60s polling)         Nano Store (chartStore)  â”€â”€â†’ Chart Card island
   162â†’```
   163â†’
   164â†’**Data interfaces:**
   165â†’- `PriceTick`: `{ price: number, timestamp: number, direction: 'up' | 'down' | 'neutral' }` â€” normalized from both Binance and CoinCap formats
   166â†’- `MarketData`: `{ marketCap: number, change24h: number, changePercent24h: number, high24h: number, low24h: number }` â€” from CoinGecko REST
   167â†’- `ChartPoint`: `{ time: number, value: number }` â€” hourly price points for lightweight-charts
   168â†’- `ConnectionStatus`: `'connecting' | 'live' | 'reconnecting' | 'stale' | 'fallback'`
   169â†’
   170â†’### Authentication & Security
   171â†’
   172â†’Not applicable. No user accounts, no API keys, no sensitive data. All external APIs are public and keyless.
   173â†’
   174â†’**Client-side security considerations:**
   175â†’- No user input to sanitize (read-only dashboard)
   176â†’- WebSocket connections use `wss://` (TLS encrypted)
   177â†’- No localStorage/sessionStorage usage â€” no data persistence to protect
   178â†’- Content Security Policy: consider adding CSP headers when deploying (Phase 2) to restrict WebSocket origins
   179â†’
   180â†’### API & Communication Patterns
   181â†’
   182â†’**WebSocket Manager (`src/lib/connection-manager.ts`):**
   183â†’- Singleton pattern â€” one instance manages the entire WebSocket lifecycle
   184â†’- Fallback chain: Binance `wss://stream.binance.com:9443/ws/btcusdt@trade` â†’ CoinCap `wss://ws.coincap.io/prices?assets=bitcoin`
   185â†’- Reconnection: exponential backoff starting at 1s, doubling to max 30s cap
   186â†’- 24h expiry: Binance hard-expires connections at 24 hours â€” handled as a standard reconnect cycle
   187â†’- Stale threshold: 30 seconds without a price update â†’ `ConnectionStatus` transitions to `'stale'`
   188â†’- Publishes normalized `PriceTick` to `priceStore` (Nano Store)
   189â†’- Publishes `ConnectionStatus` to `statusStore` (Nano Store)
   190â†’
   191â†’**REST Polling Service (`src/lib/market-data-service.ts`):**
   192â†’- Polls CoinGecko REST API every 60 seconds (within 30 req/min free tier)
   193â†’- Fetches: market cap, 24h change, 24h high/low, hourly historical data for chart
   194â†’- On API failure: retains last known data, does not clear stores
   195â†’- Publishes to `marketStore` and `chartStore` (Nano Stores)
   196â†’
   197â†’**Error Handling Standards:**
   198â†’- WebSocket errors â†’ silent reconnection via backoff, status indicator updates
   199â†’- REST errors â†’ retain stale data, log to console, no user-facing error UI
   200â†’- No thrown exceptions reaching the UI â€” all error states expressed via store values
   201â†’- Console logging for debugging only â€” no external error reporting service in v1
   202â†’
   203â†’### Frontend Architecture
   204â†’
   205â†’**State Management: Nano Stores**
   206â†’- Decision: Nano Stores (~1KB) as the reactive state layer shared across Astro islands
   207â†’- Rationale: Astro's officially recommended approach for inter-island communication. Reactive, tiny, framework-agnostic.
   208â†’- Stores:
   209â†’  - `priceStore` â€” current `PriceTick` (written by ConnectionManager)
   210â†’  - `statusStore` â€” current `ConnectionStatus` (written by ConnectionManager)
   211â†’  - `marketStore` â€” current `MarketData` (written by MarketDataService)
   212â†’  - `chartStore` â€” array of `ChartPoint` (written by MarketDataService)
   213â†’
   214â†’**Island UI Framework: Vanilla TypeScript**
   215â†’- Decision: No UI framework. Islands use Astro `<script>` tags with `client:load` for direct DOM updates.
   216â†’- Rationale: Islands perform simple operations (update text, toggle classes, call lightweight-charts API). Framework overhead adds bundle weight without proportional value. Keeps total JS well within <100KB budget.
   217â†’
   218â†’**Component Architecture:**
   219â†’- 5 Astro components as defined in UX spec: Page Shell (static), Price Card (island), Market Cap Card (island), Chart Card (island), Status Indicator (island)
   220â†’- Each island subscribes to relevant Nano Stores and updates DOM directly
   221â†’- No component nesting beyond page â†’ island â€” flat hierarchy
   222â†’
   223â†’**Bundle Optimization:**
   224â†’- Astro static output â€” only island JS ships to client
   225â†’- Nano Stores: ~1KB gzipped
   226â†’- lightweight-charts: ~40KB gzipped
   227â†’- @fontsource fonts: self-hosted, subset to Latin characters
   228â†’- Estimated total hydrated JS: ~50-60KB gzipped (well within 100KB budget)
   229â†’
   230â†’### Infrastructure & Deployment
   231â†’
   232â†’**v1: Local Development Only**
   233â†’- `npm run dev` â€” Astro dev server with HMR
   234â†’- No deployment pipeline, no hosting configuration
   235â†’- Environment configuration: API endpoints as hardcoded constants in `src/lib/` (all public, no secrets)
   236â†’
   237â†’**Phase 2 Deployment Path (documented, not implemented):**
   238â†’- Target: Vercel or Netlify (static hosting)
   239â†’- Build: `npm run build` â†’ `dist/` directory with static HTML/CSS/JS
   240â†’- No server-side requirements â€” pure static deployment
   241â†’- Consider moving API endpoints to `.env` if multiple environments needed
   242â†’
   243â†’### Decision Impact Analysis
   244â†’
   245â†’**Implementation Sequence:**
   246â†’1. Project initialization (starter commands)
   247â†’2. Tailwind `@theme` token configuration + `theme-constants.ts`
   248â†’3. Nano Stores setup (4 stores with TypeScript interfaces)
   249â†’4. ConnectionManager (WebSocket lifecycle + fallback chain)
   250â†’5. MarketDataService (REST polling)
   251â†’6. Page Shell (static layout grid)
   252â†’7. Price Card island (subscribes to priceStore + statusStore)
   253â†’8. Status Indicator island (subscribes to statusStore)
   254â†’9. Market Cap Card island (subscribes to marketStore)
   255â†’10. Chart Card island (subscribes to chartStore, configures lightweight-charts)
   256â†’
   257â†’**Cross-Component Dependencies:**
   258â†’- ConnectionManager â†’ priceStore, statusStore (writes)
   259â†’- MarketDataService â†’ marketStore, chartStore (writes)
   260â†’- Price Card â†’ priceStore, statusStore (reads)
   261â†’- Market Cap Card â†’ marketStore (reads)
   262â†’- Chart Card â†’ chartStore (reads)
   263â†’- Status Indicator â†’ statusStore (reads)
   264â†’- theme-constants.ts â†’ Chart Card (color configuration)
   265â†’
   266â†’## Implementation Patterns & Consistency Rules
   267â†’
   268â†’### Critical Conflict Points Identified
   269â†’
   270â†’12 areas where AI agents could make different choices, organized into 5 categories. All patterns below are mandatory for implementation consistency.
   271â†’
   272â†’### Naming Patterns
   273â†’
   274â†’**File Naming:**
   275â†’- Astro components: PascalCase â€” `PriceCard.astro`, `ChartCard.astro`, `StatusIndicator.astro`, `PageShell.astro`
   276â†’- TypeScript modules: kebab-case â€” `connection-manager.ts`, `market-data-service.ts`, `theme-constants.ts`
   277â†’- Store files: kebab-case â€” `price-store.ts`, `status-store.ts`, `market-store.ts`, `chart-store.ts`
   278â†’- Test files: co-located with source, `.test.ts` suffix â€” `connection-manager.test.ts`, `format-utils.test.ts`
   279â†’- CSS: single global file â€” `src/styles/global.css`
   280â†’
   281â†’**Code Naming:**
   282â†’- Variables and functions: camelCase â€” `priceStore`, `formatCurrency()`, `handlePriceTick()`
   283â†’- Types and interfaces: PascalCase â€” `PriceTick`, `MarketData`, `ConnectionStatus`
   284â†’- Constants: UPPER_SNAKE_CASE â€” `STALE_THRESHOLD_MS`, `WS_BINANCE_URL`, `REST_POLL_INTERVAL_MS`
   285â†’- Store names: camelCase with `Store` suffix â€” `priceStore`, `statusStore`, `marketStore`, `chartStore`
   286â†’- Event handlers: `handle` + noun + verb pattern â€” `handlePriceUpdate()`, `handleConnectionDrop()`
   287â†’
   288â†’**CSS/Tailwind Naming:**
   289â†’- Semantic token names in `@theme`: kebab-case with category prefix â€” `--color-accent-primary`, `--color-bg-base`, `--color-text-muted`
   290â†’- `@apply` compositions (max 3): `.card-glass`, `.price-text`, `.status-badge`
   291â†’- Astro scoped style keyframe names: kebab-case â€” `@keyframes price-flash`, `@keyframes fade-in`
   292â†’
   293â†’### Structure Patterns
   294â†’
   295â†’**Project Organization:**
   296â†’```
   297â†’src/
   298â†’â”œâ”€â”€ pages/
   299â†’â”‚   â””â”€â”€ index.astro              # Single page entry
   300â†’â”œâ”€â”€ layouts/
   301â†’â”‚   â””â”€â”€ Layout.astro             # Page shell wrapper
   302â†’â”œâ”€â”€ components/
   303â†’â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ PriceCard.astro          # Hydrated island
   304â†’â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ MarketCapCard.astro      # Hydrated island
   305â†’â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ ChartCard.astro          # Hydrated island
   306â†’â”‚   â””â”€â”€ StatusIndicator.astro    # Hydrated island
   307â†’â”œâ”€â”€ lib/
   308â†’â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ stores/
   309â†’â”‚   â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ price-store.ts
   310â†’â”‚   â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ status-store.ts
   311â†’â”‚   â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ market-store.ts

   313â†’â”‚   â”œâ”€â”€ services/
